articles.title: “You met me at a very chinese time in my life”: “China-maxxing” and the rise of intimate mediascapes
articles.authors: Yichen Rao; Lucie Chateau
articles.abstract: “Chinamaxxing” is an Internet trend emerging in late 2025–early 2026, spanning ironic memes, conspiritual content, and aspirational lifestyle vlogs. Across these practices, global users construct an ambiguous project of “becoming Chinese.” We introduce the concept of “intimate mediascapes” to theorize how contemporary platform environments intensify affective engagement with images. Extending Appadurai’s notion of mediascapes, we argue that mediated images are no longer primarily encountered as representations of distant worlds, but as habitual, immersive resources that organize everyday life. In Chinamaxxing, what circulates is not an alternative “truth” about China, but a different mode of imagining through images. Drawing on dialogic auto-ethnography, we mobilize our divergent media habitus across distinct ecosystems. We show how algorithmic attention economies and multimodal user practices generate fleeting yet powerful forms of cultural intimacy, enabling a diffuse, vernacular form of soft power grounded in affect, routine, and everyday image-making rather than state-led cultural diplomacy.

articles.title: Data quality reporting in multi-database pharmacoepidemiologic studies: a scoping review and a proposal for a harmonised classification
articles.authors: Judit Riera-Arnau; Natalia del Peso Casado; Vjola Hoxhaj; Ivan M Guzmán; Daniel R Morales; Kenneth KC Man; Jeffrey S Brown; Jason Simeone; Donna R Rivera; Miriam Sturkenboom
articles.abstract:
Objective: To map existing data quality (DQ) dimensions and to evaluate how DQ is reported in published multi-database pharmacoepidemiologic studies (MDPES) using real-world data (RWD). 

Design: Scoping review 

Data sources: PubMed, searched from 01 September 2016 to 31 July 2023.  

Eligibility criteria: Pharmacoepidemiologic, non-interventional studies about drug effectiveness, safety or use in humans, that reported information on DQ. Eligible articles were required to use more than one data source (i.e., MDPES). No language restrictions were applied. 

Review methods: Titles and abstracts were screened using ASReview, and full texts were reviewed and sampled using the SisKeyFinder text-mining tool. DQ dimensions were classified according to established frameworks. We extracted study characteristics, data combination strategies, reported DQ dimensions, and authors’ assessments of DQ impact. Descriptive analyses were conducted using RStudio.  

Results: After screening 16,087 records, we included 84 articles. MDPES publications tripled between 2016 and 2023. Overall, nearly 50% of articles primarily focused on drug utilisation, and 75% selected a cohort design. While 39% of the articles lacked explicit reporting on data combination strategies, 34.5% reported using a CDM, of which 41% were study-specific CDMs and 31% general CDMs (all the data was converted). All articles reported on extensiveness of the data source, and >85% addressed validity, relevance, reliability, and security. Conversely, data degradation, timeliness, flexibility, and usability were rarely reported. Overall, 68 (81%) of articles discussed the impact of DQ over their study results. Representative authors’ quotes are reported per dimension. We proposed a comprehensive classification of DQ concepts, structured into four domains: intrinsic, contextual, technical and cooperative. 

Conclusions: Despite absence of formal guidelines, most articles acknowledged the impact of DQ aspects to their results. Our study provides critical insights on DQ reporting practices in MDPES and offers evidence-based recommendations to guide future research and practice. Adopting structured reporting formats, checklists and guidelines is essential to improve transparency, comparability, and reliability in MDPES.

articles.title: Lost in transition. Quantifying the funding metadata gap in Crossref
articles.authors: Hans de Jonge; Katharina Rieck
articles.abstract:
The importance of having funding metadata openly available is widely acknowledged for research transparency and tracking research funding outcomes. Since 2013, Crossref has provided members the opportunity to deposit funding information when registering DOIs for publications. However, earlier research shows this information is far from complete, with coverage varying significantly across publishers despite funding information often being available in article acknowledgement sections.

This paper quantifies this metadata gap at approximately 30%. Using publications from two national funding councils (the Dutch research council NWO and the Austrian Science Fund FWF), we demonstrate that 30% of funding acknowledgements readily available to publishers in full-text articles are not transferred as metadata to Crossref. We also observe considerable differences between publishers. 

This work provides, for the first time, a concrete baseline for improving funding metadata quality and coverage in Crossref. It may also inform publishers about their performance, many of whom may be unaware of these gaps due to outsourced metadata extraction processes.

articles.title: An Empirical Assessment of Data Sharing and Computational Reproducibility in Sociology
articles.authors: Rio Ikeuchi; Taisuke Imai
articles.abstract: Public sharing of data and analysis code enables independent verification of published findings, yet systematic evidence on such sharing in sociology remains scarce, even as audits have been conducted in psychology, economics, and political science. We examine 730 empirical articles published between January 2019 and June 2024 in three leading general-interest journals: American Sociological Review, American Journal of Sociology, and Social Forces. For each article, we record whether a publicly accessible replication package is provided, and for those that share materials, we attempt to computationally reproduce the main-text tables and figures. Across the sample, 9.9% of articles provide replication packages, with substantial variation across journals (5.2% to 22.9%) and research types (0% for qualitative studies, 30% for experiments). These rates fall well below those reported for political science and economics. Among 72 packages examined in detail, more than half cannot be verified due to missing or incomplete materials. At the same time, no article is wholly non-reproducible, and 22% are fully or largely reproducible. Sociology is not unusually prone to errors when materials are shared and runnable - it simply shares them far less often. We discuss implications for journal policies and for transparency standards that reflect the field's methodological diversity.

articles.title: Open Editors Plus: A Comprehensive Dataset of Editorial Board Composition Across 15,000 Academic Journals
articles.authors: Basile Chretien
articles.abstract: Editorial boards serve as central gatekeepers in scholarly publishing, yet comprehensive, enriched data on their composition has remained scarce. We introduce Open Editors Plus, a dataset of 922,466 editorial board positions across 15,175 journals from 48 publishers, covering 619,700 unique editors in 189 countries. Each record is enriched with algorithmically inferred gender, institutional geolocation via the Research Organization Registry, bibliometric indicators from OpenAlex, and journal indexing status across five major databases. Among editors with gender-resolvable names (primarily European-origin; 30% of records are unresolved), 33% are female, with Editors-in-Chief at 25.5%. Geographic concentration is extreme (Gini = 0.90); the top 10 countries hold 71% of positions. One in four editors serves on multiple boards, and multi-board editors have substantially higher h-indices. The dataset is released under CC0 on Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19468382) with an interactive explorer at openeditors-plus.org.

articles.title: Desnutrição Funcional e o Capital Cognitivo na Amazônia Legal: Modelo Conceitual de Intervenção Integrada entre Saúde Pública e Bioeconomia
articles.authors: Walter Pereira
articles.abstract: Amazônia Legal brasileira apresenta um paradoxo epidemiológico persistente: a coexistência de uma biodiversidade singular com índices críticos de insegurança alimentar e nutricional. A desnutrição funcional infantil — caracterizada pela carência crônica de micronutrientes e proteínas em um contexto de oferta calórica aparentemente adequada — constitui um entrave estrutural ao neurodesenvolvimento e à formação do capital humano regional. A literatura científica disponível documenta os mecanismos biológicos subjacentes, mas evidencia uma lacuna relevante: a ausência de arcabouços conceituais que integrem, simultaneamente, intervenção nutricional multicomponente, validação em contextos reais de alta vulnerabilidade logística e articulação com cadeias produtivas da bioeconomia territorial. O presente artigo apresenta uma estrutura teórico-conceitual baseada em plausibilidade biológica, que articula eixos proteico, hematológico e neurocognitivo com o uso de bioativos amazônicos padronizados. Trata-se de uma hipótese estruturada de intervenção, cuja validade empírica depende de estudos de campo controlados e metodologicamente rigorosos.

articles.title: Identifying individual difference correlations when the data are noisy: A case study of proactive and reactive control
articles.authors: Jeffrey N. Rouder; Shanglin Yang; Todd Samuel Braver
articles.abstract: The Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework postulates two distinct and semi-independent modes of cognitive control.  Proactive control is hypothesized to be anticipatory, engaged prior to stimulus onset, whereas reactive control is triggered by stimulus or response features.  In the current study, we test a key prediction of this framework: that individual differences in proactive control and reactive control should be weakly correlated, if at all.  There is a key challenge faced by correlational analyses: measurement error will systematically lower correlation values; so much so that observing a small (or non-existent) correlation is difficult to interpret.  To address this challenge, we develop a hierarchical model that appropriately estimates measurement error and individual covariation simultaneously.  Drawing on a recent large-sample dataset that experimentally dissociates proactive and reactive control during the Stroop color word task (Ileri-Tayar et al, 2025), we demonstrate that hierarchical-model-based analyses not only disattenuate correlations from the impact of measurement error noise, but also provide trustworthy credible intervals that are better suited for statistical inference.  With measurement error properly estimated and removed, we find evidence that proactive and reactive control indices are only weakly related, consistent with the DMC framework.  This work showcases the advantages of hierarchical models for individual differences research, by providing a blueprint for how researchers can accurately estimate correlations as well as measurement-related uncertainty in low-reliability task contexts.

articles.title: Collective problem decomposition improves the wisdom of deliberative crowds
articles.authors: Federico Barrera-Lemarchand; Laura Victoria Lescano Charreau; Julieta Ruiz; Nuria Caceres; Facundo Carrillo; Mariano Sigman; Joaquin Navajas
articles.abstract: Understanding when and why social interaction improves human judgment is a central question in the behavioural sciences. We examine whether collective accuracy improves when groups break complex estimation problems into simpler components and generate approximate intermediate estimates, a reasoning strategy we call Collective Fermi Estimation. Across three experiments analysing more than 1,000 online group deliberations in text chatrooms, we study the causal effects and linguistic signatures of this strategy. In Study 1 (N = 500), spontaneous use of problem decomposition, as rated by human annotators, predicts lower collective estimation error. Study 2 (N = 240) provides causal evidence: groups instructed to apply problem decomposition outperform groups instructed to combine their initial guesses. Study 3 (N = 160) shows that the benefits of the method are larger when applied collectively than individually. We also introduce a fully automated approach to detect Fermi-style reasoning in conversations based on large language models (LLMs). Using five state-of-the-art LLMs, we obtain ratings that strongly correlate with human annotations and predict collective accuracy across all studies. These findings identify problem decomposition as a key mechanism behind the wisdom of deliberative crowds and provide tools to detect and promote it.

articles.title: Youth First Supports Adolescent Wellbeing: A Longitudinal Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial of a Teacher-Delivered School-Based Program in India
articles.authors: Peter L. Cooper; Katherine S. Leventhal; Gunjika Misra; Nandita Bhatla; Hari Shanker; Steve Leventhal
articles.abstract:
Globally, adolescents are facing a “polycrisis” of intersecting challenges that place them at heightened risk for poor mental health outcomes, particularly in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), where 90% of the world’s adolescents live. School-based wellbeing programs offer a promising, cost-effective prevention strategy, yet evidence from large-scale, government-delivered interventions with longer-term follow-ups remains limited. This study evaluates the effectiveness of Youth First, a teacher-delivered, in-school wellbeing program, as conducted in Bihar, India, via government systems, addressing key evidence gaps relevant to LMIC government adoption.

We conducted secondary analysis of data from a cluster-randomized controlled trial across 69 government schools in Bihar, India, enrolling 5,445 Grade 7–8 students. Schools were randomized to Youth First or control (standard education). Data were collected at baseline, 8 months, and 20 months. Outcomes measured included physical and mental wellbeing, psychosocial assets, gender attitudes, empowerment, and tobacco use. Analyses followed an intention-to-treat framework using mixed-effects difference-in-differences models with random intercepts for schools and students.

Youth First led to statistically significant improvements in social and peer support across multiple time points and cohorts. Additional significant effects were observed for self-efficacy, empowerment, internalizing symptoms, psychological wellbeing, and tobacco use, with patterns varying by grade and exposure duration. Notably, several effects persisted or intensified one year post-intervention, including for psychological wellbeing and self-efficacy.

This longitudinal cluster-randomized controlled trial demonstrates that a scalable, government-delivered school wellbeing program can produce meaningful and sustained benefits across multiple priority outcomes for adolescents in an LMIC setting.

articles.title: Movement-related differences in infants with high and low likelihood of autism: A longitudinal study using automated kinematic tracking
articles.authors: Saber Sotoodeh; Georgina Donati; Ori Ossmy; Hannah Rowan; Gillian Forrester
articles.abstract:
Aim: While motor differences are increasingly recognised as early signs of autism, research on the developmental trajectories of specific kinematic features remains limited. This study aimed to examine the early emergence of specific kinematic features (duration, range, velocity, acceleration, entropy, and jerk) in infants with high and Low-Likelihoods of developing autism.
Method: We longitudinally evaluated weekly, home-recorded videos of naturalistic, spontaneous movement from 60 infants (comprising 705 total videos) across the first six months of life. Automated pose estimation (computer vision) was utilised to extract objective kinematic data from the shoulders, elbows, hips, and knees, while controlling for environmental and postural variables.
Results: Analysis revealed significant group differences in the variability of movement entropy. Specifically, the variability of kinematic entropy was significantly lower in the high-likelihood cohort compared to the low-likelihood cohort throughout the first six months of infancy.
Interpretation: These results indicate a reduced complexity in early spontaneous motor behaviour among high-likelihood infants. Detecting these early kinematic differences via scalable computer vision may provide a quantifiable, objective marker of the neural atypicalities associated with autism prior to the onset of traditional diagnostic behaviours.

articles.title: Dissociable contributions of working memory capacity and lapses of attention to cognitive deficits in schizophrenia
articles.authors: Sonia Bansal; Kirsten C. S. Adam; John Kiat; Gi-Yeul Bae; Benjamin Robinson; Steven J Luck; James Gold
articles.abstract: Working Memory (WM) impairment is a central feature of schizophrenia, yet it remains unclear whether WM deficits primarily reflect reduced storage capacity or inconsistent attentional engagement of available capacity. Traditional trial-averaged measures obscure this distinction. In this study, we combined a multi-item visual WM task with computational modeling to distinguish maximum storage capacity from trial-to-trial consistency of attentional engagement in people with schizophrenia (PSZ, N=52) and healthy control subjects (HCS, N=41). Participants completed two task variants that differed in response demands: a Free Response Order condition that permitted self-selected report order and a Random Response Order condition that imposed an externally determined order. PSZ showed robust impairments in average WM performance across tasks. Model-based analyses revealed that these impairments reflected both a reduction in maximum WM capacity and reduced consistency of attentional engagement. Capacity estimates were stable across task contexts, whereas consistency was selectively reduced in PSZ when response order was externally imposed. Time-resolved analyses demonstrated that group differences were stable across the experimental session and were not attributable to learning or fatigue effects. These findings indicate that WM deficits in schizophrenia arise from distinct impairments in both storage capacity and attentional consistency, and that task context influences the expression of these deficits. Parsing these components advances mechanistic models of cognitive dysfunction in schizophrenia and may help guide the design of cognitive assessments that are more sensitive to underlying process-level impairments.

articles.title: Considering psychological mechanisms can change the interpretation of Bayesian models
articles.authors: Elizabeth Bonawitz; Thomas L. Griffiths
articles.abstract: A significant challenge for Bayesian models of cognition is understanding how the abstract assumptions behind these models connect to psychological mechanisms. We examine the consequences of considering one of the psychological processes involved in inductive inference: generating hypotheses. We analyze the predictions of a simple model that separates the processes of generating hypotheses and evaluating those hypotheses. This analysis shows that the ease of generating a hypothesis and its plausibility are confounded if we simply analyze people’s behavior in terms of Bayesian inference without taking the underlying mechanisms into account. We then establish through three experiments that the processes of generation and evaluation are separable, and that influencing the ease of hypothesis generation has different consequences from simply manipulating the plausibility of a hypothesis. These experiments produce results that could not be accounted for by a Bayesian model that assumes people assign a fixed prior probability to hypotheses.

articles.title: Comparing physical literacy scores by sex and movement behaviour distributions across physical literacy profiles among elementary school students: an exploratory study
articles.authors: Henri Moncanis; Pierre-Yves de Müllenheim; Nicolas Hauw
articles.abstract: Current literature supports the idea that physical literacy may serve as a key concept for promoting, in children, physically active lifestyles over the long term. However, in France, few studies have focused on physical literacy levels among children, particularly when it comes to comparing scores between boys and girls. Building on a North American approach, the Canadian Assessment of Physical Literacy Second Edition (CAPL-2) framework provides classification of physical literacy profiles based on the overall physical literacy score across four main dimensions: physical, behavioural, affective, and cognitive. The association between these profiles and relevant indicators of movement behaviour distribution remains unclear in the literature. Therefore, the present exploratory study had two main aims: (i) to compare physical literacy scores by sex; and (ii) to compare, across physical literacy profiles, indicators of movement behaviour distribution (intensity gradient, tendency to spend time at higher intensities, and power-law exponent alpha, sedentary accumulation patterns). Ninety-nine final-year primary school students (Avrillé, Maine-et-Loire, France) were assessed using the CAPL-2. CAPL-2 total scores, multivariate combinations of CAPL-2 domain and item scores by sex, and multivariate combinations of movement behaviour distribution metrics across physical literacy profiles were compared using nonparametric analyses. The results encourage conducting confirmatory studies focusing primarily on sex differences in overall physical literacy and the affective dimension. It also supports conducting studies confirming the ability of physical literacy profiles to reflect the intensity gradient, that is, children's tendency to spend their activity time at higher intensities.

articles.title: Enhancing Test-Taker Experience in Computerised Adaptive Testing: A Comparison of Constraint Methods for Regulating Item Difficulty Jumps
articles.authors: Chia-Wen Chen; Joe Watson; David Stillwell; Vesselin Popov
articles.abstract: Computerised Adaptive Testing (CAT) leverages Item Response Theory (IRT) to provide efficient, individualised assessments. However, traditional CAT algorithms often produce abrupt "item difficulty jumps", sudden increases in difficulty between consecutive items, which can trigger test anxiety and frustration. This study proposes four methods to regulate these jumps: Fixed Start Item Design (FS), Constrained Item Difficulty Jumps (CDJ), Constraint on Difficulties Lower than Ability (CDLA), and Weighted Fisher Information (WFI).Through two simulation studies, one theoretical and one using empirical data from 13,701 students, the methods were evaluated for measurement accuracy, efficiency, and difficulty stability. Results indicate that while item difficulty converges in 1PL models, it frequently exhibits large, persistent jumps in 2PL models under traditional selection criteria. Among the proposed methods, CDJ effectively restricted jumps within a 0.5 logit threshold without significantly sacrificing measurement precision or increasing test length. CDLA demonstrated high efficiency when item banks contained a surplus of easy items, making it suitable for lower-ability or vulnerable populations. Conversely, WFI provided the strictest jump control but at a substantial cost to measurement efficiency at extreme ability levels. FS performed poorly, particularly for high-ability test-takers. The study concludes that CDJ is recommended as a robust default for 2PL-based assessments, while CDLA is preferable for item banks with predominantly easy content. Future research should experimentally validate the psychological impact of specific logit-scale difficulty jumps.

articles.title: Collapsing Waste Fractions Without Reducing Sorting Accuracy
articles.authors: Anton Prip Wrisberg; Annika Wallin; Philip Pärnamets
articles.abstract: Waste sorting signs present an inviting avenue for comparison of categorisation theories as they often contain both a rule and either an abstract prototype or visual exemplars. Focus in waste sorting studies is often on increasing the number of waste fractions, but some public settings might require fewer-than-ordinary fractions. This paper investigates two approaches to reducing a waste sorting system’s granularity at a music festival: explicit combining waste fractions by merging waste signs and implicitly combining fractions by removing alternatives to ‘residual waste’. The digital sorting results are modelled as a probit regression allowing direct interpretation in terms of signal detection theory’s senstitivy and criterion parameters. The paper further compares four alternative waste sorting signs including visual exemplars to improve waste sorting accuracy for the implicitly combined waste fraction. The results suggest that explicitly combining waste fractions outperform implicit combinations – even with visual exemplars added, despite resulting in more false positives.

articles.title: From Features to Objects: Developmental Differences in Reinforcement Learning Strategies
articles.authors: Moira Decorte; Mina Habibi; Senne Braem
articles.abstract: Humans constantly learn in complex environments, and a central challenge is deciding whether to attend to individual features or whole objects – a learning strategy that may shift with development. Here, we investigated developmental differences between children and adults in biases toward these two forms of learning. Participants (children aged 8–12 and adults) completed a reinforcement learning task in which both strategies had benefits, allowing us to model and directly compare them across age groups. Both groups showed clear signs of learning, but adults obtained more rewards than children. Interestingly, adults showed a clear preference for object-based learning, whereas children appeared to arbitrate equally between the two strategies. Dynamic model comparisons further showed that although both groups gradually shifted from feature-based to object-based learning over time, this transition was more pronounced in adults. Children, by contrast, relied longer on feature-based learning and focused more on features predictive of reward. Together, these findings suggest that developmental changes in reinforcement learning efficiency are driven by prolonged reliance on feature-level representations early in life, with increasing age marked by a shift toward more efficient, object-centered strategies. Importantly, these differences reflect the granularity of learned representations rather than differences in learning rate, highlighting how humans extract structure from complex reward environments across development.

articles.title: Social media reveals population dynamics of dysphoric dreaming
articles.authors: Remington Mallett; Laura Sowin; Michelle Carr
articles.abstract: Nightmares disrupt sleep health and predict future psychiatric diagnoses, yet reliable population estimates and their fluctuations over time are difficult to obtain. Here, we set out to test if a digital surveillance approach could be used to observe reliable estimates of nightmare frequency and dysphoric dreaming in the general population from social media posts. English-language posts from r/Dreaming, a subreddit where users share their dreams, were analyzed using Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count to quantify nightmare frequency and anxious dreaming from text. An interrupted time series analysis was used to show that both nightmare frequency and dysphoric dreaming increased immediately following the World Health Organization's declaration of COVID-19 as a global pandemic. This observation mirrors a large body of existing work that used more traditional methods. This "digital dream surveillance" approach might offer the field of sleep medicine a low-cost and real-time system for monitoring population sleep health.

articles.title: Mapping Extreme Deviations in Cognition to Transdiagnostic Compulsivity: A Normative Modelling Approach
articles.authors: Alice Cox; Gabriel Byczynski; Kelly Rose Donegan; Anna Hanlon; Vanessa Teckentrup; Paveen Phon-Amnuaisuk; Martina Sladekova; Claire Gillan
articles.abstract: Transdiagnostic compulsivity has been linked to reductions in model-based planning, cognitive flexibility and working memory. However, it’s unclear if these effects reflect dimensional variation or asymmetric effects at the extremes. To address this, we used normative modelling to identify extreme positive and negative deviations in cognitive performance using data from N=10,328 participants who completed three smartphone-based tasks. Individuals were stratified into model-derived cognition categories (extreme low: <10th percentile, moderate: 10th-90th percentile, extreme high: >90th percentile), and differences in compulsivity scores across groups were assessed via regression. We report disproportionately elevated compulsivity scores among participants with extreme negative deviations, but no commensurate decrease in those with extreme positive deviations. Compulsivity norms revealed disproportionally reduced cognitive performance among participants with extreme positive deviations, but no corresponding increase in those with extreme negative deviations. These findings provide a nuanced understanding of directional differences driving clinical-cognitive relationships, further refining mechanistic accounts of compulsivity.

articles.title: Self-Control as a Mediator Between Personality Traits and Subjective Well-Being: Evidence from Three Panel Studies
articles.authors: Kehan Mei; Sakari Lemola; Yuxuan Su; Anu Realo
articles.abstract:
Objective
Research suggests that self-control may mediate the relationships between the Big Five personality traits and subjective well-being (SWB), yet its role remains unclear. 
Method
The mediating effect of self-control (measured at Time 2 (T2) on the relationships between the Big Five personality traits (measured at Time 1 (T1)) and components of SWB (measured at Time 3 (T3)) were examined in three longitudinal panel datasets of which three assessment waves were considered: HILDA (Australia, N = 11,737, Mage at T1= 46.19), SOEP (Germany, N = 1,268, Mage at T1= 52.94), and MIDUS (United States, N = 2,614, Mage at T1= 46.30). The intervals from T1 to T3 were 4 years in HILDA, 5 years in SOEP, and 20 years in MIDUS. 
Results
Self-control mediated the associations of Neuroticism and Conscientiousness with life satisfaction across all samples, and with positive and negative affect in HILDA and MIDUS samples. The mediating role of self-control varied by sex, with Extraversion demonstrating a significant pathway for women but not men in HILDA.
Conclusions
These findings indicate that self-control links personality traits to SWB, with potential implications for intervention.

articles.title: A One-Stage Approach for Estimating a Meta-Analytic Continuous-Time Random-Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model
articles.authors: Julian F Lohmann; Oliver Lüdtke; Alexander Robitzsch
articles.abstract: This article describes, evaluates, and applies a meta-analytic random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM). The model enables meta-analytic study of temporal relations between two variables while separating stable between-person differences from within-person fluctuations. The RI-CLPM has been proposed as an alternative to the cross-lagged panel model and has recently motivated many reanalyzes of longitudinal datasets. To support flexible, integrative reanalysis across multiple datasets, we introduce a Bayesian one-stage approach for fitting a meta-analytic RI-CLPM using correlation matrices from longitudinal primary studies. The method combines Bayesian meta-analytic structural equation modeling with continuous-time modeling. The continuous-time framework allows us to account for different time lags across studies, a common challenge in longitudinal meta-analysis. We propose four model variants that differ in their random-effects structures for capturing between-study heterogeneity. A simulation study compares their performance, and an empirical example from educational psychology examines reciprocal relations between academic self-concept and achievement, illustrating their application.

articles.title: From Courtrooms to Ballots: A Social Ecological Analysis of Anti-Trans Judicial Outcomes on Legislation, Elections, and Trans Geographic Demography
articles.authors: Margaret Stevenson; Kyla Bender-Baird
articles.abstract: We applied a social ecological model to examine whether judicial decisions denying trans plaintiffs’ claims in employment discrimination cases forecast anti-trans legislative growth, and in turn, Trump support and LGBT and trans geographic demography. That is, we explore associations between anti-trans actions in three branches of government. Specifically, we coded (a) a census of trans employment discrimination judicial case outcomes (N = 124) decided from 1974 to 2021, (b) the number of anti-trans bills each state introduced, (c) state electoral support for Trump, and (d) state LGBT population density and trans geographic relocation. Supporting hypotheses, states where judges disproportionately denied trans plaintiffs’ employment discrimination claims were more likely to introduce anti-trans legislation, which in turn, was associated with increased likelihood of state electoral support for Trump, lower state LGBT population density, and greater trans geographic relocation. Refuting the notion of an independent judiciary, judicial decisions in trans employment discrimination cases forecasted the drastic growth in anti-trans legislation, and in turn, executive branch outcomes. This research suggests that a social ecological theoretical model may offer a useful framework for understanding the influences and consequences of anti-trans legislation.

articles.title: A qualitative systematic literature review of the lived experiences of people with depersonalisation-derealisation disorder
articles.authors: Rebecca Shashoua; Helge Gillmeister; Emilia Halton-Hernandez
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Despite depersonalisation-derealisation being the third most commonly reported psychiatric symptom, it remains chronically understudied and poorly understood. Research into depersonalisation-derealisation disorder (DDD) is largely quantitative and remains scarce. This systematic literature review is the first review to synthesise available qualitative research about DDD lived experiences to understand the nature and impact of the disorder’s symptoms. The findings are interpreted in light of the cognitive behavioural model of DDD.  

Methods: Five electronic databases (APA PsycArticles, APA PsycInfo, CINAHL Ultimate, open dissertations, MEDLINE Ultimate) were searched alongside Google Scholar from their inception until September 2025 using a combination of search terms for depersonalisation, derealisation, lived experiences and qualitative methods. The methodological quality of studies was assessed using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme Tool for qualitative research and data was analysed using Thomas and Harden’s (2008) approach to thematic synthesis. 

Results: Twelve studies were identified as eligible for inclusion. Thematic synthesis revealed five themes across participants’ accounts: (i) Struggling to Navigate Distortions in Time and Sensory Perception; (ii) Changes in Identity, Roles and Beliefs; (iii) Navigating Societal Stigma and Altered Relationships; (iv) Negative Affective Responses: Anxiety, Numbness, Suicidality; and (v) Challenges to Daily Functioning. Most studies demonstrated high methodological quality, with only one exception. 

Discussion: The lived experiences presented in this review provide context to the impact of DDD symptoms on individuals and those around them. It underscores the need for holistic, person-centred interventions that address not only symptom reduction but also broader issues not otherwise captured by standardised measures of DDD symptoms surrounding self-perception, emotional wellbeing, relational difficulties, altered sensory perception and functional recovery. Findings broadly support the cognitive behavioural model of DDD, while also indicating important areas of divergence, thereby extending current theoretical understanding of the condition.

articles.title: Age-related changes in how previous learning experiences impact subsequent visual statistical learning and relate to executive functions - a longitudinal study.
articles.authors: Matthew Daniel Johnston; Jessica Dolan; Bence Csaba Farkas; Nicolas Chevalier
articles.abstract: Through statistical learning (SL), children implicitly extract repeated patterns in their environment. The developmental trajectory of how previously learned statistical information impacts children’s subsequent learning is poorly understood. Furthermore, how SL contributes to other developmental processes, like executive function, has received surprisingly little attention. In an accelerated longitudinal design, we assessed 187 5-12-year-olds’ (42.8%= female, 84.6%= White) SL and executive function capacities over 16 months. Cue-based SL and retention of nonadjacent probabilistic sequences was age-invariant. However, whilst all age groups showed an increased sensitivity to deterministic sequences with each successive timepoint, 5-6-year-olds demonstrated a greater longitudinal increase in sensitivity than older children. This finding suggests enhanced abilities to apply previously learned deterministic sequences to novel scenarios. Regarding SL-executive function links, greater sensitivity to adjacent conditional regularities at Time 2 predicted executive function increases between Times 2-3 for 8-9-year-olds, though this effect was weak and absent in younger and older groups. Whilst further work is needed to explore SL-executive function links across other modalities, our results suggest that early visual SL capacities may not be a primary driver of executive function change during middle childhood.

articles.title: Urban versus Rural Living and Health Outcomes: A Global Synthesis of Evidence Across Multiple Domains
articles.authors: Johanna Prugger; Izabela Maria Sztuka; Simone Kühn
articles.abstract: Urbanization has raised urgent questions about how environmental contexts shape mental and physical health. This review synthesizes findings from 500 studies (1982–2023) on mental health, physical health, brain function, cognition, personality, and epigenetics in urban versus rural settings. In the Global North, urban living is linked to increased risks for mental disorders, particularly schizophrenia, whereas rural living is often associated with better quality of life. In the Global South, urban environments may confer mental health benefits, likely due to improved healthcare and economic opportunities. Urban populations, mostly in the North, show higher rates of obesity and cardiovascular disease, while rural groups face greater mortality risks linked to healthcare disparities. Neuroimaging studies suggest urban upbringing alters stress-related brain circuits. However, causal inferences are limited by cross-sectional designs, inconsistent definitions of “urban” and “rural,” and overrepresentation of Global North populations. Improved classification and global representation are needed to inform equitable and effective health policy.

articles.title: Beyond Snapshots: A Systematic Review of Dyadic and Triadic Parent-Adolescent Ecological Momentary Assessment Studies Across Diverse and General Populations
articles.authors: Reuma Gadassi Polack; Martine Wilhelmina Francina Teresia Verhees; Reout Arbel
articles.abstract: The last decade has seen a surge in studies using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), where participants complete brief daily questionnaires to capture real-time experiences. While widely used to study adolescents' social-emotional processes, EMA that simultaneously assesses adolescents and their parents (i.e., dyadic and triadic designs) remains underutilized, particularly within diverse families. This gap is surprising given the proven value of dyadic EMA in studying adult relationships and its potential to elucidate contextualized family dynamics. This systematic review synthesized and critically evaluated existing EMA research in parent-adolescent dyads and triads (i.e., an adolescent and both parents) published between 2014 and 2024, examining methodological and analytical practices as well as representation of diverse populations (i.e., racial/ethnic, sexual and gender minorities, lower-SES, non-traditional family structures, and clinical populations). Following a systematic search of electronic databases, we identified 85 publications originating from 44 distinct datasets that conducted EMA in parent-adolescent dyads or triads. Substantial variability was observed across studies in the number of daily assessments, EMA days, and data-waves collected. Utilization of the multilevel and dyadic features of the data also varied: most papers fully utilized the temporal aspect (e.g., examining within-family processes), while the dyadic/triadic aspect was only partially utilized (e.g., most studies examined unidirectional effects, typically parent-to-child). Regarding populations studied, while most focused on White, non-Hispanic, highly educated samples, some targeted only minority groups (e.g., Chinese-American). However, whether within-family processes differ between populations was rarely examined. Across dyadic (vs. triadic) studies, fathers were underrepresented, and gender identity and sexual orientation were almost never assessed, likely masking the exclusion of gender and sexual minorities. Parent-adolescent dyadic and triadic EMA appears feasible and could provide unique insights into family dynamics in typical and diverse populations. Gaps in the literature and suggestions for advancing inclusion and diversity are discussed.

articles.title: The skewed distribution of educational technology research - a sign of academic hegemony? An analysis of localization in scientific publications
articles.authors: Katja Buntins; Svenja Bedenlier
articles.abstract: Academic knowledge is interwoven with historical power structures, leading to global ine-qualities in the visibility of research, and favouring studies from the Global North. Articles from the Global North are significantly less likely to incorporate geographic markers in their titles compared to those addressing the Global South. We examine the prevalence of regional markers in the titles of 39,764 educational technology research studies from 81 Scopus-indexed journals between 2015 and 2024. Only 11.4% of study titles indicate a geo-graphical region. If so, articles from sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America show the highest localization rate, and Europe and North America the lowest. Multiple regression analysis uncovers a negative correlation between per capita GDP and localization rates, suggesting that as countries develop economically, the tendency of articles being localized decreases. Publishing conventions may thus reinforce epistemic hierarchies by favouring studies that appear to be more widely applicable, relegating others to regional relevance.

articles.title: Revisiting Cross-Modal Matching in the Light of Luce’s Global Psychophysical Theory
articles.authors: Katharina Naumann; Jürgen Heller
articles.abstract: In this high-powered replication of the classical cross-modal matching experiment by Stevens and Marks (1965, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the USA, 54, 407-411) examining loudness and brightness, we complement the traditional group-level analysis with an analysis at the individual level, and model the underlying mechanisms utilizing the theory of global psychophysics with internal references. In cross-modal matching, one typically finds a shortened range of the adjusted stimulus exhibiting a tendency towards a mid-level intensity. This well-replicated result is called regression effect.  We implement a novel Bayesian estimation of the global psychophysics model for cross-modal matching.  In a sample of seven subjects, the regression effect is found both at the aggregated and the individual level. The observed prevalent nonlinearity of the individual matching curves can be captured by the global psychophysics model.  Implications and further directions are discussed.

articles.title: On cross-modal commutativity in magnitude production for loudness and brightness
articles.authors: Dorina Kohler; Jürgen Heller
articles.abstract: Can the loudness of a tone be compared to the brightness of a light? The answer is affirmative. We appear to be intuitively capable of making such cross-modal comparisons. Psychophysical research has long assumed that these comparisons are mediated by a common scale of subjective intensity shared across modalities. R.D. Luce (2002, Psychological Review, 109, 520-523) developed a psychophysical theory of magnitude estimation and production based on this assumption and derived empirically testable conditions. In particular, he identified cross-modal commutativity as a key property for empirically evaluating the theory. In an extended, high-powered replication of an experiment reported by W. Ellermeier, F. Kattner, and A. Raum (2021, Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 83, 2955-2967), the present study examined this property using a cross-modal magnitude production task for loudness and brightness. Relative to the previous study, individual-level analyses yielded more substantial evidence both for and against crossmodal commutativity, while group-level results remained inconclusive. These findings are discussed in light of a generalized theory that predicts a near-miss to cross-modal commutativity while preserving the assumption of a shared representation of subjective intensity.

articles.title: Adaptive Cognitive Control in Prospective Memory
articles.authors: Luke Joseph Gough Strickland; David Elliott; Shayne Loft; Niek Stevenson; Andrew Heathcote
articles.abstract: Event-Based Prospective Memory (PM) requires performing a planned action upon encountering a target event. The PM decision control (PMDC) model quantifies the dual mechanisms of cognitive control (proactive and reactive, Braver, 2012) that support PM. Here, we test a key prediction of the dual-mechanisms theory: that cognitive control will shift from proactive to reactive as the need for control lessens due to PM targets being infrequent. Participants made lexical decisions (ongoing task) and were presented blocks of trials which either contained no PM targets, a high PM target frequency (19% of trials), or a low PM target frequency (5%). We fit the behavioural data with an extended version of PMDC that was augmented to specify the ongoing-task habituation and PM learning dynamics required to account for the way in which participants adapted their behaviour as a function of task practice and expected PM target frequency. PM costs (ongoing task response slowing in PM conditions relative to no PM target condition) were observed only in the high frequency condition, explained by PMDC by an increase in proactive control. PM accuracy was similar across high and low frequency conditions early in the experiment, but later an advantage emerged for the lower frequency PM task. PMDC accounted for this with stronger reactive control in low frequency condition that increased over practice. Our results confirm the prediction that control shifts from proactive to reactive with lower target event frequency, respond to recent calls from the PM literature for theory to account for across-trial dynamics.

articles.title: Quests, Challenges, and Clinical Change: A Review and Clinical Recommendations for the Use of Therapeutically Applied Role Playing Games for people with Psychosis.
articles.authors: Terry Rae; Sana Pathan; Poppy Watson
articles.abstract: While the interest in Therapeutically Applied Role-Playing Games (TA-RPGs) is growing globally, there is a reluctance from clinicians and researchers to apply this intervention with people who experience psychosis. Understandably, there are concerns that a fantasy-based game will exacerbate psychotic symptoms. This review assesses the evidence relating to the risks and benefits of implementing TA-RPGs with people with a lived experienced of psychosis. Despite these concerns, there is no evidence that participation in TA-RPGs exacerbates symptoms for those who experience psychosis. Instead, emerging evidence highlights the benefits of engaging in TA-RPG interventions for many participants, regardless of the presence of psychotic symptoms. We highlight the need for more transparency and research in this area to reduce the research-to-practice gap and address stigma for those experiencing psychosis. Finally, we provide clinical recommendations to increase safety when implementing TA-RPGs with this population.

articles.title: Comparing Compensatory and Non-Compensatory MIRT Models at Scale: Evidence from IRW
articles.authors: Yiqing Liu; Lijin Zhang; Benjamin Domingue
articles.abstract: A central choice in multidimensional item response theory (MIRT) concerns how multiple latent skills combine to produce success: compensatory models allow trade-offs across dimensions, whereas fully noncompensatory models impose a conjunctive constraint in which limited proficiency on any required dimension can constrain success. Despite longstanding conceptual interest, there is limited evidence on how these skill-combination assumptions affect out-of-sample predictive performance across large-scale assessments. Using the Item Response Warehouse (IRW) and a common missing-responses cross-validation framework, we compare a unidimensional 2PL baseline (1D-2PL) with compensatory and fully noncompensatory two-dimensional 2PL specifications (C2PLM and FNC2PLM) and summarize predictive differences with the InterModel Vigorish (IMV), a portable metric for binary outcomes. A simulation motivates conservative scale thresholds for stable predictive comparisons, after which we conduct empirical analyses across multiple large-scale dichotomous assessments. Across datasets, the compensatory specification yields small (mean IMV = 0.00154) but consistently positive predictive improvements over the unidimensional baseline, whereas the fully noncompensatory specification rarely improves prediction and is seldom preferred over the compensatory alternative (mean IMV = -0.00475). Item-level analyses further reveal within-test heterogeneity, suggesting that conjunctive structure tends to be localized to subsets of items rather than test-wide. These results provide an empirical base-rate reference for comparing skill-combination assumptions under missing-responses prediction and motivate follow-up work that expands to additional assessment programs, relaxes inclusion thresholds to increase dataset diversity, and evaluates alternative prediction targets.

articles.title: Toward a Formalization of Human Intuitive Theories of Bodily Pain
articles.authors: Marlene Berke; Katherine M Collins; Joshua Tenenbaum; Rebecca Saxe
articles.abstract: Pain is a subjective experience that people often communicate through words. How do observers, from family members to medical professionals, interpret these reports? We propose that people use causal inference to infer someone else's subjective pain experience, treating the pain experience as an unobservable latent variable and the report as one of several sources of observations. We formalized this as Bayesian inference over a causal model of bodily pain and tested it against human judgments on naturalistic stimuli drawn from Reddit posts. Critically, the model is stimulus-computable, taking as input exactly what participants see. The model quantitatively predicted human judgments about pain intensity and appropriate painkiller dosage and generalized across different kinds of pain (e.g., from headaches to finger burns). Furthermore, the model captured individual differences correlated with people's explicit beliefs about how best to assess pain. This work takes a first step toward a scientific understanding of folk intuitive theories of pain.

articles.title: Psychometric Evaluation of Online Sexual Abuse Indicators in the UNICEF Disrupting Harm Project
articles.authors: Matthew Richards; Sakshi Ghai; Amy Orben
articles.abstract:
This analysis examined the psychometric properties of nine items related to online sexual exploitation and abuse, as well as follow-up questions on disclosures and reporting, from the Disrupting Harm questionnaire. Both two- and three-factor structures were identified through exploratory factor analyses and validated using confirmatory factor analyses. Each model demonstrated good fit, with adequate item loadings and no cross-loadings. 
The three-factor model aligned conceptually with pre-registered factors, labelled ‘risk of sexual exploitation’ (four items), ‘risk of sexual abuse’ (three items), and ‘risk of sexual harassment’ (two items). In contrast, the two-factor model combined ‘risk of sexual abuse’ and ‘risk of sexual harassment’ into a single factor, ‘risk of sexual harassment and abuse’ (five items), with ‘risk of sexual exploitation’ (four items) remaining separate.
Reliability for the two-item ‘risk of sexual harassment’ factor in the three-factor model was low, consistent with psychometric literature indicating that three items are typically required for reliable measurement. Consequently, further analyses, including measurement invariance and item response theory, focused on the two-factor model, which demonstrated good reliability for all factors. The two-factor model showed strict invariance across sex, strong invariance across age, and strong invariance across countries after applying a log transformation to resolve convergence issues.
Item response theory on the two-factor model indicated that the factors were most informative at average trait levels, providing the highest measurement precision for respondents with moderate levels of responses across each of the nine items. However, two items (captured by the ‘risk of sexual harassment’ factor in the three-factor model) provided comparatively lower measurement precision into the broader ‘risk of sexual harassment and abuse’ factor (encompassing both ‘risk of sexual harassment’ and ‘risk of sexual abuse’). This suggests that these items may reflect a distinct theoretical construct that is better captured in the three-factor model. 
Both two- and three- factor structures are presented in this analysis, each with limitations. The two-factor model demonstrates greater psychometric validity but may obscure a distinct construct that the three-factor model captures, albeit with insufficient reliability. To reconcile theoretical relevance with psychometric robustness, future research should consider adding an item to the ‘risk of sexual harassment’ factor and adopting a three-factor structure. 
Descriptive analyses indicated that 27% (n = 2,859) of the sample reported at least one abusive experience. Among this group, 66% (n = 1,884) reported only ‘risk of sexual exploitation’, 29% (n = 817) reported both ‘risk of sexual harassment and abuse’ and ‘risk of sexual exploitation’, and 6% (n = 158) reported only ‘risk of sexual harassment and abuse’.

articles.title: Switching to an Allocentric Spatial Reference Frame Incurs Distinct Cognitive Processing Costs
articles.authors: Michael James Starrett Ambrose; Craig Stark; Liz Chrastil
articles.abstract: The ability to acquire, recall, and flexibly use information from first-person (egocentric) and map-like (allocentric) reference frames is essential for human wayfinding and thus, daily life. Prevailing models of the neural systems that support the representation and coordination between reference frames suggest that switching back-and-forth should result in either lower accuracy, slower response time (RT), or both. However, predictions of these models have not been adequately tested for spatial memory of large-scale, naturalistic environments. Inspired by previous work on task switching, we developed a novel task in which participants performed a spatial memory switching task that included trials in both egocentric and allocentric reference frames. Participants learned a large virtual neighborhood by navigating to various target stores. They then completed runs of the memory task, with approximately equal numbers of egocentric and allocentric trials. Critically, we manipulated whether the same reference frame was repeated or switched on a trial-to-trial basis to determine whether there is a cost associated with changing reference frames. Results showed no significant differences in spatial memory accuracy across conditions but significantly slower RTs for switching trials. Importantly, this reference frame switch cost was only observed for egocentric-to-allocentric switching (not vice versa) relative to all other conditions. These findings suggest that the cognitive process of reference frame switching is asymmetric, which went against our predictions from neural models. Future neuroimaging studies to test predictions generated by our results will provide crucial insight into human spatial memory function.

articles.title: A Proof-of-Concept Trial of a Culturally Adapted Virtual Reality-Cognitive Behavioural Therapy with Inuit in Montreal
articles.authors: Quinta Seon; Roxanne Drainville; Liliana Gomez Cardona; Joy Outerbridge; Stéphane Bouchard; Outi Linnaranta; Martin Lepage
articles.abstract:
Background: A lack of  access to culturally sensitive psychotherapy detrimentally impacts the mental health and wellbeing of Inuit in Quebec. Previously, we reported a co-creation process of a culturally adapted cognitive-behavioural therapy manual that incorporates salient cultural imagery with virtual reality (VR-CBT). In this proof-of-concept trial, we aimed to examine the feasibility and acceptability  of our VR-CBT, and suitability of research methods for a future larger trial.
Methods: We ran a mixed-method, one-arm proof-of-concept longitudinal trial (ISRCTN21831510) to assess feasibility. Some aspects of the protocol were changed in the initial phases to increase cultural and logistical alignment. Inuit living in Montreal who were interested in therapy and tolerated the VR headset attended a ten-week cognitive-behavioural therapy with a psychotherapist. They provided biweekly measures of  emotion regulation, psychological wellbeing, and psychological symptoms. Feasibility markers were enrolment and attrition, with complementary qualitative interviews and questionnaires. Exploratory markers of effectiveness were emotion regulation, psychological wellbeing, and psychological symptoms. 
Results: We recruited twenty participants, which matched our expectations. Ten Inuit attended at least one therapy session, with the majority completing five of the ten sessions. The recruitment, enrolment and conduct of the therapy itself were promising. However, attrition was high, randomization was unfeasible, and several aspects of the original protocol modified. The results indicated a trend towards improvement in outcomes. 
Conclusions: Our trial demonstrates the promise of delivering culturally adapted and technology-assisted therapy. We undertook a collaborative research process, recruitment, and therapy delivery, and gained knowledge of how to improve the feasibility of culturally sensitivity digital mental health. The project highlighted the challenges of conventional trial designs required by funders in evaluating effectiveness of psychotherapy in Indigenous  contexts. 
Trial Registration Number: International Standard Randomized Controlled Trial Number (ISRCTN) 21831510; https://www.isrctn.com/ISRCTN21831510

articles.title: Content Validation Using Natural Language Processing: A Comparative Examination of Models for Multi-Item and Single-Item Measures
articles.authors: Fabio Alivernini; Mariangela Di Nallo; Tommaso Palombi; Mattia Samory
articles.abstract: Content validation, the assessment of whether measurement items adequately capture their intended constructs, is essential to scale development in organizational research but remains resource-intensive when conducted through conventional human-rating procedures. Natural language processing (NLP) offers a scalable complement for estimating definitional correspondence and distinctiveness, yet no study has systematically compared the full range of available approaches against direct human validity judgments across both multi-item and single-item measurement formats. The present study evaluates 16 NLP models spanning five architectural families (statistical models, static word embeddings, contextual sentence embeddings, task-specific interaction models and large language models) using two independent human-rated benchmark datasets. Results indicated that no single approach dominated across validity components or measurement formats, and that computational complexity was neither necessary nor sufficient for predictive accuracy. For multi-item correspondence, contextual sentence embeddings achieved top-group accuracy alongside supervised content-validation models; the choice of similarity metric substantially moderated performance for static embeddings. For multi-item distinctiveness, a broad upper group spanning all major architectural families achieved statistically indistinguishable accuracy. Single-item correspondence revealed a task-imposed performance ceiling with no architectural advantage. Practical recommendations are derived for each use case, prioritizing reproducibility, interpretability, and generalizability alongside predictive accuracy. A freely available web application implementing the recommended pipeline is introduced.

articles.title: The Strength of Target Representations Drives Mnemonic Discrimination at Extended Delays
articles.authors: Brock Kirwan; Samuel Chipman; Martina Krisztina Hollearn; Cory S Inman
articles.abstract: Mnemonic discrimination, or the ability to distinguish between similar remembered experiences, is critical for episodic memory and is dependent on the computational process of pattern separation. Prior work with tasks that place high demands on mnemonic discrimination such as the Mnemonic Similarity Task (MST) has focused on short retention intervals and it remains unclear how mnemonic discrimination evolves over longer delays. To address this gap in understanding, we examined MST performance at delays ranging from a few minutes to seven days in two experiments. Consistent with prior forgetting literature, both recognition of repeated items and discrimination of similar lures declined with delay. In the immediate delay condition, the similarity of lure stimuli to targets drove discrimination performance, with more similar lures eliciting false alarms and less similar lures eliciting correct rejections, as reflected in the lure discrimination index (LDI). This graded relationship was attenuated or absent at delays longer than one day. However, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analyses revealed preserved discrimination across delays and target-lure similarity levels. Additionally, we observed an inversion of the typical confidence-accuracy relationship for lure trials at longer delays, with low-confidence responses more likely to be correct especially for high-similarity lures. Together these findings reflect the influence of the strength of target representations on lure discrimination: strong target representations lead to high-confidence correct rejections of low-similarity lures but also to high-confidence false alarms to high-similarity lures. As target representations weaken over extended delays, false alarms to high-similarity lures become less likely while low-similarity lures were more likely to be called “new”. This is the first demonstration of how mnemonic discrimination in the MST changes over extended delays.

articles.title: Preregistration records do not reliably reflect the true state of psychology meta-analyses
articles.authors: Anastasia Bredenbrock; Daniel S Quintana
articles.abstract: Meta-analysis preregistration is intended to promote transparency and reduce research waste. However, it is largely unknown whether preregistration records accurately reflect the actual state of research, and how many preregistered meta-analyses are ultimately published in the field of psychology. We searched PROSPERO for preregistrations of psychology meta-analyses registered between 2011 and 2021 (n = 1137), verified publication status through systematic search, and contacted corresponding authors of unpublished registrations (n = 358) to obtain information on reasons for delays and discontinuations. We found that PROSPERO status labels were systematically inaccurate: 57% of records labelled "ongoing" were already published, and publication rates varied by registration status (Completed: 94%; Ongoing: 59%; Discontinued: 9%). Among 80 survey respondents, resource limitations were the primary reasons for project discontinuation (lack of time: 47%; lack of personnel: 11%; lack of funding: 7%) and preregistration quality showed no association with publication likelihood. Altogether, these findings suggest that PROSPERO registrations in psychology frequently fail to reflect the true status of a meta-analysis, undermining their value as a transparency tool, and that resource limitations frequently lead to meta-analysis abandonment. We recommend that journals and funders require registration updates at publication to reduce the accumulation of inaccurate and abandoned records.

articles.title: What’s on Their For You Page? A Large-Scale Computational Approach to Analyzing Adolescents’ TikTok Archives Through Hashtag Topic Modeling
articles.authors: Amber van der Wal; Inga Vondenhof; Konrad Mikalauskas; Rebecca Godard; Kfir Zioni; Felicia Loecherbach; Ine Beyens
articles.abstract: Short-form video platforms like TikTok play a central role in adolescents’ lives, yet little is known about the actual content they engage with. This study presents a large-scale, computational approach to analyze adolescents’ TikTok feeds using hashtag-based topic modeling applied to user-donated archives from 102 Dutch adolescents (ages 15–19). Using BERTopic, we identified 304 coherent topics, organized them into 32 higher-order content categories, and validated both through a structured human validation study. Analyses reveal that adolescents’ feeds are dominated by entertainment and leisure content, with ‘Media productions,’ ‘Sports,’ ‘Style and appearance,’ ‘Hobbies,’ and ‘Gaming’ as the most prevalent categories. At the same time, there is marked individual variation: niche areas such as K-pop, anime, and specific fandoms are central to some users’ feeds. Latent profile analysis identified six distinct, gendered content-consumption profiles. Together, the findings shed light on adolescents’ TikTok exposure, revealing predominantly benign but highly personalized media diets in which appearance-focused content stands out as a potential risk domain. In all, our approach offers a scalable, efficient way to map short-form video content. Linking these maps to individual users’ media diets opens new possibilities for examining how different content patterns relate to user experiences and outcomes.

articles.title: The psychology of cringe
articles.authors: Paul Deutchman; Shaon Lahiri
articles.abstract: While cringe is an increasingly common term in social discourse and popular media—often used to describe a situation perceived as awkward or uncomfortable—we currently understand little about its underlying psychology and function in human social life. Across five studies (N = 1416; three preregistered), we examined the social cognition of cringe as a stable individual difference trait and as a fleeting affective state, investigating whether it is the same as, or functionally distinct from, vicarious embarrassment. We propose that cringe is a specific variety of vicarious embarrassment elicited by intentional, reputationally damaging social norm violations, that functions as a signal of normative competency—communicating recognition and alignment with group norms—and motivates norm compliance and enforcement. We first developed and validated the novel 20-item Cringe Scale to measure individual differences in trait cringe which we found positively predicted state cringe perceptions, incentivized donations, and behavioral intentions. Additionally, we found that cringe, as compared to vicarious embarrassment, was elicited more intensely by intentional and public norm violations, and was associated with heightened moral wrongness and punishment judgements as well as reduced empathy and prosocial motivation toward the transgressor. Path analyses revealed that perceptions of transgressor intent and norm violation severity mediated these differences. Together, our results establish cringe as a functionally distinct variety of vicarious embarrassment characterized by psychological distancing rather than empathic concern. The findings inform our understanding of an increasingly important self-conscious emotion and illuminate the adaptive social functions of cringe in social cognition.

articles.title: Effectiveness of a School-based Workshop Promoting Mental Health on Adolescents Mental Health Literacy and Emotional Self-Efficacy
articles.authors: Joana Rupprecht; Tina Wendel; Salka Beck; Markus Grill; Matthias Ziegler; Bettina K. Doering; Anke Haberkamp
articles.abstract:
Theoretical background: Mental health crisis in adolescents is a growing concern in society and research. Because of the high prevalence of mental disorders during adolescence and the long waiting times for access to therapeutic services, low-threshold, school-based prevention programs are needed that empower adolescents to actively advocate for their mental health. The aim of this study was to assess the effectiveness of a one-day school-based mental health workshop on mental health literacy (MHL) and emotional self-efficacy (ESE) within a quasi-randomized delayed cross-over design. 

Method: Data was collected from 868 students (age: 14-17 years, 39.3% female) in six schools between March 2024 and February 2025 at three data assessment points. Participants in the intervention group (n1 = 365 (M₁ = 14.8 (0.86), 54.8% female) participated in a one-day school-based mental health workshop provided by the organization Kopfsachen e.V. between the baseline-assessment (T0), 2-3 weeks before the workshop and the post-assessment (T1) directly after the workshop. There was a follow-up assessment (T2) around six months after the baseline (T0). Participants in the waitlist-control group (n2 = 503 (M₂ = 15.0(0.85)) participated at all three assessments and received the workshop after the follow-up assessment (T2).  

Results: The workshop had significant short-term effects (T0-T1) on all MHL and one ESE variable within the overall group comparison, with the strongest effect on professional help-seeking-intentions (β = 0.42, p <.001, η²ₚ = .04). However, these effects were not sustained until the follow-up assessment (T3). Explorative Exploratory analyses revealed significant moderating effects of gender, age, personal experience, circumstantial experience, and personal relevance. Qualitative data revealed that the needs and expectations of the adolescents before the workshop largely matched to their take-home messages after the workshop. 

Conclusions: This study confirms the short-term effectiveness of a one-day school-based mental health workshop on adolescents’ MHL and ESE. Fading effects over time underscore the need for tailored interventions, booster sessions, and continued research to ensure sustainable long-term impact.

articles.title: Patterns of registered and self-reported sleep problems in young adults with different histories of childhood adversity
articles.authors: Jette Echterhoff; Tjeerd Rudmer Vries; Adrian G. Zucco; Katrine Strandberg-Larsen; Levente Kriston; Naja Hulvej Rod
articles.abstract:
Background: Sleep problems are increasing among young adults, with those experiencing childhood adversity particularly at risk. This study provides a comprehensive overview of sleep problems in young adults following multifaceted trajectories of childhood adversity, while emphasizing the transdiagnostic nature of sleep problems.

Methods: Register data on 1,276,831 individuals were supplemented with nested survey data on 36,873 participants. Childhood adversity (ages 0-16) was operationalized using previously derived trajectories: low adversity, early life material deprivation, persistent material deprivation, loss or threat of loss, and high adversity. Sleep problems (ages 16-30) were assessed through prescriptions, diagnoses, and self-reported data. Survival models were used to estimate associations between childhood adversity and sleep problems. Co-occurrence of sleep and mental health problems were explored, and confirmatory factor analysis was used to model self-reported sleep impairment across adversity groups.

Findings: Childhood adversity was associated with sleep problems, with prevalences of 8∙2%, 10∙4%, 10∙8%, 13∙6%, and 20∙1% across the five adversity groups. Risk was highest in the high adversity (HR 2∙37; 95% CI 2∙32–2∙43) and loss or threat of loss (1∙71; 1∙68–1∙74) groups vs. low adversity. Sleep problems most commonly involved sleep medication prescriptions only (49%) but also co-occurred with mental disorders (43%). The high adversity and loss or threat of loss groups showed higher variation in self-reported sleep impairment and higher proportions above the 80th percentile compared with other groups.

Interpretation: Sleep problems are common among young adults who experienced childhood adversity; linking early-life stress, disturbed sleep, and mental health conditions from childhood into adult life. These interconnected public health challenges underscore the relevance of multi-sectoral policy approaches, including upstream prevention of childhood adversity and downstream recognition and treatment of sleep problems, while considering variability in how these problems develop, manifest, and persist.

Funding: Lundbeck Foundation (grant no. R396-2022-352) and Tryg Foundation (grant no. 157232).

articles.title: “It Feels Like We are an Outsider”: Understanding Barriers and Creating Opportunities for Fathers’ Engagement in Parenting Programs
articles.authors: Carolina Garraio; Dora d'Orsi; Marc Grau-Grau; Paula Mena Matos; Marisa Matias
articles.abstract: Becoming a father entails significant psychological adaptations that impact fathers’ well-being and mental health, but also that of their partners and children. As such, structured parenting programs may play an important role in supporting fathers during this transition. However, fathers remain underrepresented in these programs, and studies consistently report challenges in recruiting and engaging them. These difficulties suggest a potential misalignment between parenting programs and fathers’ needs, expectations, and sociocultural contexts, often shaped by their broader experiences with family- and health-related services. This study aims to explore how Portuguese fathers perceive and position themselves in relation to parenting programs, and to identify the social, cultural, and institutional factors that shape their engagement and non-engagement. We conducted four focus groups with 16 fathers who had at least one biological child under five years old. The focus groups were moderated by two psychologists (one male and one female) and followed a semi-structured script. Using reflexive thematic analysis, six themes were developed: fathers as secondary parents – experiences and perceptions of exclusion; generational gaps; gap between service awareness and willingness to engage; perceived legitimacy of fathers in parenting support spaces; seeking a place of belonging; design elements supporting fathers’ engagement. Overall, fathers’ engagement emerges as a contextually embedded process shaped by institutional practices, cultural expectations, and perceived legitimacy. Enhancing participation may entail fostering inclusive environments that recognize fathers as central caregivers. This calls for broader transformations of cultural and institutional frameworks that shape parenting support from the earliest stages of a father’s parenthood trajectory.

articles.title: How and when to implement self-efficacy interventions to maximize the benefits of virtual reality exposure therapy for fear of heights
articles.authors: Kayleigh Piovesan; Julia Klenke; Armin Zlomuzica
articles.abstract: Strategies aimed to enhance self-efficacy (SE), such as mastery experiences and verbal persuasion may promote exposure therapy. Here, we evaluated the effects of two strategies to boost SE administered during and/or after virtual reality exposure therapy (VRET) for fear of heights. Height-fearful participants (N = 83) were randomly assigned to one of four conditions: VRET alone, VRET combined with SE enhancement via positive performance feedback during VRET, VRET combined with post-exposure SE via mastery experience activation and evaluation, or VRET combining both SE enhancement strategies. Subjective, behavioral, and psychophysiological indices of height fear and general SE were assessed from pre- to post-intervention and at 3-month follow-up. Across conditions, height fear and avoidance decreased while behavioral approach increased with effects largely maintained at follow-up. Participants receiving post-exposure mastery experience activation and evaluation showed the strongest reductions in subjective fear during the behavioral approach test and in anxiety expectancies. Decrease in subjective height avoidance was also stronger compared to the condition that received SE enhancement during VRET. Notably, participants receiving positive performance feedback during VRET showed the least improvement (across several outcome measures). Our results indicate that post-exposure activation and evaluation of mastery experiences is suitable to further promote VRET outcome in height fear. Conversely, applying positive performance feedback during VRET seems ineffective and leads to diminished exposure treatment benefit. These findings provide novel perspectives on the potential use of SE enhancement as a supplementary strategy to augment exposure therapy. This study was preregistered on ClinicalTrials.gov (ID: NCT05824884).

articles.title: Psychometric Validation of a Deep Learning-based Foreground Speech Detection Algorithm for Everyday Conversation Detection
articles.authors: Amanda Marie Bernal; Johannes Leonhard Klinz; Valeria Pfeifer; David Sbarra; Charles L Raison; Nicole Nugent; Rajat Hebbar; Shrikanth Narayanan; Matthias R. Mehl
articles.abstract: Passive ambient audio sampling bears great potential for objectively measuring daily social activity and its association with wellbeing and health. However, detecting true conversations via human annotation is labor-intensive, and automatic labeling via audio signal processing has thus far only received proof-of-concept validation.
