Although there exist well-validated measures of metacognitive efficiency (i.e., the degree to which confidence ratings distinguish between correct and incorrect decisions), relatively less attention has been paid to developing corresponding measures of metacognitive bias (i.e., the overall tendency to make responses with high confidence). Most researchers currently use mean confidence as a proxy for metacognitive bias, however this measure is confounded by changes in accuracy, response bias, and metacognitive sensitivity. Here, we introduce a new signal detection theoretic measure of metacognitive bias (meta-delta) and demonstrate that it is independent of a range of potential confounds. Then, by analyzing longitudinal data, we show that this measure is reliable enough to use in psychological research. Overall, we advocate for the use of meta-delta as a principled measure of metacognitive bias.

articles.title: Word Order and Information Status Effects in German: An Expectation-based Perspective
articles.authors: Torsten Kai Jachmann; Francesca Delogu; Matthew Crocker; Heiner Drenhaus
articles.abstract: In languages with flexible word order, diverse constraints contribute to comprehenders' expectations regarding the linearization of constituents. In German for example, subjects typically precede objects, while given information typically precedes new information. Violations of either preference are known to incur processing costs. The present study investigates how German comprehenders incrementally integrate these sources of expectancy when both syntactic role and information status are unambiguously cued by each noun phrase. In addition, we examine how referents with intermediate discourse accessibility -- that is, referents that are not explicitly mentioned but are implied by the context -- are represented in discourse. Across five self-paced reading experiments, we find that word order and information status jointly influence processing effort at the first noun phrase, indicating that linearization expectations are fully updated as soon as grammatical role and discourse accessibility cues become available. Crucially, the cost associated with object-initial structures is attenuated when the object is discourse-given, suggesting that contextual support can license dispreferred linearizations. Finally, implied referents pattern more closely with new than with given referents, although the effects elicited by such entities appear to depend on noun phrase definiteness as well as on contextual relevance. Overall, these findings support expectation-based accounts of linearization preferences grounded in both syntactic structure and information status, demonstrating that comprehenders rapidly integrate these sources of expectancy to guide incremental sentence processing.

articles.title: A cross-linguistic investigation of infant familiar word recognition across ages - The ManyBabies-AtHome: Looking-While-Listening project
articles.authors: Rajalakshmi Madhavan; Katie Von Holzen; Silvia Benavides-Varela; Naomi Havron; Julien Mayor; Barbara Pomiechowska; Martin Zettersten; Eon-Suk Ko
articles.abstract: The current study aims to examine the developmental trajectory of word recognition in infants learning different languages across different ages, using an online adaptation of the traditional Looking-While-Listening paradigm. Specifically, we aim to examine whether children’s word recognition as indexed by speed and accuracy of their looking toward a named referent varies as a function of (1) age of the child, (2) whether their caregiver indicated that they understand the word, and (3) the language they are learning. Infants between 10-24-months from various countries and learning various languages will be recruited by research teams across the world to participate in the study, with the goal of 48 infants for each language. Children will be presented with two familiar objects on the screen while hearing the label of one of the objects embedded in a carrier phrase. Additionally, caregivers will also report children’s knowledge of the presented words through a questionnaire. We predict that infant word recognition accuracy and speed will improve as they grow older. However, given the mixed evidence for convergence between parent reports and children’s word recognition, and the possibility that there may be variation in the relationship between caregiver report and performance across languages due to linguistic differences, the nature of the association between parental report of word knowledge or language background on children’s word recognition remains an open empirical question. With our approach, we aim to build a more inclusive model of studying infant word recognition, and to develop more resource-friendly adaptations for cross-linguistic research.

articles.title: Social camouflaging among autistic adolescents in Japan: Diﬀerential associations with aﬃliate stigma reported by mothers and fathers
articles.authors: REIRI NAMURA; Siqing Guan; Aya Taniguchi; Sophia Guan; Fumiyo Oshima
articles.abstract:
Autistic adolescentsʼ use of social camouflaging, defined as strategies used to appear less visibly
autistic during social interaction, has been increasingly recognised by researchers, but its
developmental and family context remains poorly understood. Aﬃliate stigma, experienced by family
members because of their association with an autistic person, and internalised stigma, reflecting the
incorporation of negative societal beliefs about autism into oneʼs self-concept, may be relevant
correlates. Using an online questionnaire survey of 50 autistic adolescent‒mother‒father triads, this
study examined whether parental aﬃliate stigma was associated with autistic adolescentsʼ
internalised stigma and, in turn, with social camouflaging, while comparing maternal and paternal
pathways. Adolescents reported social camouflaging and internalised stigma, and parents reported
aﬃliate stigma. Path analysis showed that autistic adolescentsʼ internalised stigma was positively
associated with social camouflaging. Both maternal and paternal aﬃliate stigma were positively
associated with adolescentsʼ internalised stigma, but neither was directly associated with social
camouflaging. In the simultaneous model, only the paternal path remained independently associated
with adolescentsʼ internalised stigma.

articles.title: Questionable prospective effects between physical activity, exercise adherence, and adverse psychological states: A comment on Xiang et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: Recently, Xiang et al. reported statistically significant effects in a cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) model and concluded a complex dynamic interplay between physical activity, exercise adherence, and indicators of adverse psychological states. However, it is well known that such cross-lagged effects may be spurious. Here, we fitted alternative models to data simulated to resemble the data used by Xiang et al. and found increasing, decreasing, and null effects between the study variables, depending on the analyzed model, and meta-analytic aggregations of these discrepant effects did not tend to differ significantly from zero. Hence, the findings by Xiang et al. may have been spurious and their conclusions premature. It is important for researchers to bear in mind that correlations, including cross-lagged effects, in observational (i.e., non-experimental) data may be spurious in order not to overinterpret findings. We recommend researchers to scrutinize cross-lagged effects by fitting alternative models to data and to base conclusions on a juxtaposition of findings.

articles.title: More like Guidelines than Hard Rules: Bilinguals Accept Lexical Overlap Despite Using Mutual Exclusivity
articles.authors: Ming Yean Sia; Julien Mayor
articles.abstract: Mutual exclusivity (ME), the tendency that young children have to select a novel object upon hearing a novel label, gives children a basis to select a referent for an unknown label. However, this strategy may not be relevant to bilinguals who learn multiple labels for most objects. Although previous research showed that bilinguals are better than monolinguals in accepting second labels for familiar objects, these studies explicitly labelled the familiar objects with a novel name, leaving open the question of whether children implicitly accept lexical overlap in ambiguous naming situation. We investigated to what extent monolingual and bilingual children associate a novel label with a familiar, name-known object when a novel object is present using a tablet-based referent selection task. Subsequently, we examined whether children retained the novel word-object association as well as whether they formed associations between the novel labels and the familiar, name-known objects. We tested 123 monolingual and 131 bilingual children between 3 and 10 years of age. We found that monolinguals and bilinguals relied on ME but bilinguals to a lesser extent than monolinguals. Monolinguals retained novel word-object associations but not the associations between novel words and familiar objects, while bilinguals retained the associations between novel words and familiar objects but not novel word-object associations. Our study extends the proposition that bilinguals are open to lexical overlap even in ambiguous referent selection situations. Our findings thus indicate that the influence of language experience on children's word-learning strategies persists throughout the first decade of life.

articles.title: Mind wandering impedes statistical learning during  serial but not parallel visual search
articles.authors: Philippe Blondé; Michał Domagała; Marcin Leszczyński
articles.abstract:
Mind wandering is a common mental state typically associated with task disengagement due to attention being shifted away from external stimuli (perceptual decoupling). However, recent work has proposed that reduced engagement of top-down executive control during mind wandering may facilitate automatic processes such as statistical learning. Here, we tested this possibility by assessing how mind wandering influences the extraction of spatial regularities during visual search. We conducted two experiments that differed in attentional demands: an attentive, serial visual search task (Experiment 1) and a pre-attentive, parallel visual search task (Experiment 2).
In experiment 1, forty-seven participants performed a probabilistic cueing task in which they searched for a T-shaped target among L-shaped distractors. Target locations were determined by a Markov chain such that one quadrant had a 70 % probability of containing the target, with the high probability quadrant dynamically determined by the location of the previous target, creating a probabilistic sequence of target locations. In experiment 2, fifty participants completed a similar task in which the target was a red dot among green dot distractors, minimizing the need for focused attention. In both experiments, participants intermittently reported their attention state via 20 thoughts probes, rating their current attentional focus from 1 (Completely on-task) to 10 (Completely off-task).
Across experiments, responses were faster when targets appeared in the high probability quadrant, which was also associated with a greater probability of a first saccade toward that location in experiment 1. Contrary to previous findings, however, mind wandering was associated with reduced statistical learning in the attentive search task (Experiment 1) and had no detectable effect in the pre-attentive search task (Experiment 2). These results suggest that the relationship between mind wandering and statistical learning depends on attentional demands: the detrimental effect observed in attention-demanding search was abolished under pre-attentive, parallel search conditions, arguing against a general facilitating role of offline attentional states.

articles.title: Empirical Findings on the Accuracy of Intellectual Self-Perceptions Depend on the Method Used to Assess Self-Perceived Ability
articles.authors: Ida Schulze Tast; Steffen Nestler; Sarah Humberg
articles.abstract: Research on people’s tendencies to over-, under-, or accurately estimate their abilities relies on assessments of self-perceived ability. Here, we investigated how the choice of an assessment method influences the conclusions drawn in research on two perspectives on accuracy. First, studies using the rank-order perspective are concerned with the correlation between self-perceived ability and an objective measure of “real” ability across individuals. Second, studies using the level perspective view the discrepancy between a person’s levels of self-perceived and real ability as an indicator of the degree of bias (vs. accuracy) and investigate the average discrepancy across people, individual differences in people’s discrepancies, or the association between the individual discrepancies and some outcome variable. In a cross-sectional survey of 281 participants, we assessed self-perceived intelligence in two domains (vocabulary knowledge, reasoning ability) with three measures each (percentile rank, IQ score, number of correct items). “Real” ability was measured with objective tests and transformed to match the scales of the self-assessments. We found that the measure for self-perceived intelligence impacted the conclusions drawn in analyses within the level but not the rank-order perspective. We discuss implications for the robustness of past findings and for the assessment of self-perceived intelligence in future studies.

articles.title: Music Curiosity Increases the Willingness to Pay for Information: From the Laboratory to the Concert Hall
articles.authors: Gemma Cardona; Xim Cerda-Company; Jacqueline Gottlieb; Enrico Sozza; David Cucurell Vega; Laura Ferreri; Antoni Rodriguez-Fornells
articles.abstract: Curiosity drives information-seeking behavior and shapes memory, yet its role in real-world contexts remains underexplored. This study addresses this question by investigating music-driven curiosity and its influence on exploration and long-term memory across laboratory and real-world (concert) settings. In Experiment 1, 63 participants engaged in a new exploration/exploitation paradigm assessing willingness to pay (WTP) to explore unfamiliar music. In Experiment 2, 150 participants attended a live DJ set, rating their curiosity and WTP for real-world musical experiences. Music-driven curiosity increased WTP and enhanced long-term memory, with stronger effects when exploration incurred potential costs, suggesting that curiosity biases decision-making by selectively promoting information seeking when potential costs are at stake. Furthermore, a machine learning classifier trained on laboratory data effectively predicted exploration behavior in the natural setting, highlighting the robustness of the results across contexts. By integrating controlled and real-world environments through an innovative paradigm, this study sheds light on the cognitive and motivational dynamics of curiosity in the aesthetic domain, providing novel insights into its potential to enhance learning and memory in applied settings.

articles.title: Autistic student well-being indicators at school: Parent, educator and therapist perspectives
articles.authors: Annette Carroll; Dawn Adams; Kate Simpson
articles.abstract: Autistic students experience disproportionately high levels of distress and unmet support needs at school, yet there is limited school‑based evidence to guide how their well‑being is recognised in everyday educational contexts. Most existing research has focused on home, community, or clinical settings, leaving a gap in understanding how autistic students communicate both high and low well‑being during the school day. This study addresses this gap by increasing understanding of school‑based indicators of autistic student well‑being across emotional, social, physical, academic, and future‑focused domains. Using a two‑phase, multi-informant design, Phase 1 collected qualitative data from caregivers, educators, and school‑based therapists (n = 41) to identify externally observable indicators of high and low well‑being. Phase 2 invited a separate sample (n = 36) to evaluate the perceived reliability of the most frequently identified indicators. Inductive content analysis identified 94 high and 99 low well‑being indicators. Three key findings emerged. First, indicators varied substantially in how they were expressed across students, highlighting the importance of interpreting change relative to individual baselines rather than normative expectations. Second, many indicators reflected specific dimensions of well‑being and did not function as global markers across contexts. Third, indicators most frequently reported were not always those judged to be most reliable indicators of autistic students’ well-being, underscoring the limitations of frequency‑based approaches. Together, these findings demonstrate that autistic student well‑being at school is best understood as multidimensional, context‑dependent, and individually patterned. By clarifying how well‑being indicators are identified and interpreted in school settings, this study provides an important foundation for future practice‑focused tools, professional learning, and research aimed at more responsive and inclusive support for autistic students.

articles.title: Cognitive Feelings from Healthy Experience to Psychopathology: An Integrative Review Across Phenomenology, Psychology, Neuroscience, and Computation
articles.authors: Keisuke Suzuki; Yuri Terasawa; Yoshiyuki Nishio; Yuichi Yamashita
articles.abstract: Cognitive feelings, such as the feeling of knowing, familiarity, presence, reality, confidence, agency, and body ownership, are subjective experiences arising from cognitive processes, playing crucial roles in perception, memory, and decision-making. Despite extensive research across philosophy, psychology, and neuroscience, they have been studied in fragmented ways, lacking a unified theoretical framework. This review integrates multiple perspectives on cognitive feelings. First, we catalog fifteen cognitive feelings organized in thirteen subsections, examining their normal functions and clinical manifestations when disrupted. Second, drawing on the phenomenological tradition from William James through Husserl and Heidegger to Ratcliffe, we distinguish three modes of cognitive feelings: feelings of thought (metacognitive monitoring), feelings of perception (constitution of worldly appearances), and feelings of being (existential self–world relations). Third, from the perspective of affective psychology, we demonstrate that cognitive feelings share neural substrates with emotions, particularly the anterior insular cortex and anterior cingulate cortex, and are bidirectionally modulated by mood states. Fourth, from a neurological perspective, we extend Damasio’s somatic marker hypothesis to cognitive feelings as metacognitive somatic markers, whose selective disruption through right-hemisphere damage produces characteristic monothematic delusions, suggesting hemispheric asymmetries. Fifth, we present a computational framework based on predictive processing theory, explaining cognitive feelings through prediction errors, precision weighting, and free energy minimization. Across these perspectives, we examine impairments in psychiatric and neurological disorders, including depersonalization, schizophrenia, Capgras syndrome, obsessive-compulsive disorder, anxiety, and depression, bridging theory with clinical observation.

articles.title: The Future of New Technology and Artificial Intelligence in Mental Health: Prospects, Challenges, and a Roadmap for Personalized Treatment
articles.authors: Nan Qiu; Duoduo Xu; Barbara Sahakian; Christelle Langley; Roland Eils; Laura Marciano; Christian Montag; Jodi Halpern; B.T. Thomas Yeo; Paul YIP
articles.abstract:
Background. Artificial intelligence (AI) and related digital technologies are rapidly entering mental health care, psychosomatic medicine, and psychotherapy. Yet the clinical value of these tools cannot be judged by technical performance alone. The field is characterized by diagnostic heterogeneity, reliance on language and self-report, complex biopsychosocial causation, and substantial variability in response to interventions. Clinically meaningful progress therefore requires integration of biological markers with patients’ lived experience, personal history and context, alongside person-centered precision decision-making and intervention.
Summary. This review examines how AI may contribute to mental health care across multiple levels, including diagnostic assessment, biomarker development, treatment selection, AI-enabled non-invasive neuromodulation, closed-loop adaptation, and emerging chatbot-based interventions. We argue that AI should support and improve, not replace, clinical assessments and care by integrating complex data into person-centered care, while raising challenges of validation, safety, equity, commodification and governance.
Key Messages. AI can strengthen and improve clinically effective, context-sensitive, and person-centered mental health care. AI may enhance diagnosis, treatment selection, and therapeutic options while preserving the human-human therapeutic alliance. The future of AI-enabled mental health depends on combining computational precision with human judgment, ethical oversight, and clinical relevance to improve patient outcomes and quality of life. 
Keywords: Artificial intelligence; Mental health; Personalized treatment; Psychotherapy; Psychosomatic medicine

articles.title: Successful Modal Reasoning Depends On WHAT Went WHERE
articles.authors: Peter Mazalik; Justin Halberda
articles.abstract: Quantifying over possibilities requires Modal Reasoning (e.g., categorizing events as necessary, possible, and impossible). This suite of abilities matures during the preschool years, but toddlers succeed at reasoning in some possibility tasks and fail at others. We propose that these mixed findings reflect a cognitive distinction: toddlers fail when tested about ambiguous locations of objects (WHERE), and succeed when tested about ambiguous identities of objects (WHAT). In Experiment 1 and 2, we directly compared these forms of ambiguity using matched tasks. Consistent with the hypothesis, toddlers succeeded at reasoning about ambiguous identities but failed when the ambiguity concerned location. Experiment 3 replicated this asymmetry in language comprehension as toddlers successfully comprehended modal terms like “can” and “have to” in an identity task but not in a location task. Thus, Modal Reasoning emerges earlier than suggested, and diversity in results reflects the contributions of distinct cognitive systems.

articles.title: From Maternal Depression to Autism Risk: Shared Neurobiological Mechanisms Across the Perinatal Period
articles.authors: Lucila Torres; Verónica de la Fuente
articles.abstract:
Perinatal depression (PND) is a subtype of major depressive disorder with onset during the peripartum
period or up to one year after childbirth in clinical practice. It is the most prevalent complication
during the postpartum period with a global prevalence between 10-20%. Emerging research has
shown that PND can exert long term effects on child development through the disruption of both
biological signaling (e.g., oxytocinergic and GABAergic system dysfunction, HPA axis dysregulation
and inflammatory state) and maternal-infant social bonding. These disruptions have been linked to
adverse cognitive, social, and behavioural outcomes, including impaired emotional attachment.
Consequently, there is an increasing imperative to evaluate the potential association between maternal
PND and the development of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) in offspring, particularly in light of the
rising global prevalence, mostly due to higher awareness and increased diagnostic recognition.
While recent literature has explored this association using mainly observational prospective studies, a
comprehensive synthesis of the evidence is currently lacking. This review aims to analyze available
literature regarding the association between PND and the development of ASD in the offspring,
focusing primarily on a possibly shared pathophysiology, which includes alterations in the
oxytocinergic and GABAergic system together with epigenetic changes and the inflammatory profile.

articles.title: Developmental and environmental differences in the first year of life of infants with family history of autism and/or ADHD in Brazil
articles.authors: Elizabeth Shephard; Thayna Dayane Pereira Barbosa Mendes; Nádia P Slwczuk; Carolina C Abati; Caroline Andrade; Karen D Moreira; Luaê HR Santos; Vitoria Quinteiro; Beatriz Y Minagawa; Karen Fernandes
articles.abstract: Prospective longitudinal studies have established that infants with family history (FH) of autism or attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) are likely to experience a range of differences in their early development. However, the vast majority of evidence concerning the early development of these infants has come from high-income-country (HIC) contexts and has not considered the role of psychosocial environmental factors. In this study, we investigated developmental and environmental differences in a large cohort (N=252) of infants aged 6-12 months (mean=8.7 months) with FH of autism (n=36), ADHD (n=66), autism and ADHD (n=84) or without FH (n=66) in a low-and-middle-income-country (LMIC) setting, Brazil. Infants with FH of autism showed significantly lower adaptive communication, motor and daily living skills as well as lower temperamental perceptual sensitivity and vocal reactivity than infants without FH of autism (F>4.18, p<0.04), while infants with FH of ADHD showed significantly higher temperamental activity levels and lower temperamental regulatory capacity than infants without FH of ADHD (F>9.23, p<0.003). Compared to infants without FH, infants with FH of autism and/or FH of ADHD had significantly higher levels of caregiver mental health difficulties and household chaos (F>7.91, p<0.005). Our findings suggest distinct profiles of developmental differences associated with FH of autism versus FH of ADHD and shared environmental vulnerabilities in the home. This work highlights the importance of providing support for infants with FH of autism and ADHD and their families in Brazil.

articles.title: Word Familiarity Norms as a Test Case:  Toward Methodological Standards for Word Norming Research
articles.authors: Maria Montefinese; Erin Michelle Buchanan; Irene Di Pietro; Giada Viviani; Silvia Benavides-Varela; Ettore Ambrosini
articles.abstract: Normative databases of word properties are a fundamental resource in experimental psycholinguistics, enabling control of lexical variables and comparability across studies. However, the methodological practices used to construct these resources remain heterogeneous, limiting their reproducibility and cumulative value. In this methodological study, we examine how subjective word experience has been operationalized, using familiarity and subjective frequency norms as a test case to evaluate broader practices in word norming research. We systematically reviewed 61 studies across 15 languages, analyzing operational definitions, rating instructions, participant samples, data cleaning procedures, psychometric validation, and data accessibility. Our analysis reveals substantial conceptual and methodological inconsistencies. In particular, “familiarity” is frequently used as a label for distinct constructs, including subjective encounter frequency, conceptual knowledge, or hybrid judgments. Beyond these conceptual issues, we identify structural limitations affecting the robustness and representativeness of existing norms, including reliance on student samples, uneven cross-linguistic coverage, incomplete methodological reporting, and limited data accessibility. At the same time, recent studies show improvements in methodological rigor, reliability assessment, and open science practices. Building on these findings, we propose evidence-based recommendations and a practical checklist to improve transparency, comparability, and inclusiveness in word norming research. We further introduce a scalable framework for future norm development that integrates AI-generated estimates with targeted human validation, enabling broader lexical coverage while maintaining theoretical interpretability. These contributions provide a foundation for improving the development, evaluation, and integration of word norming resources in a more cumulative and reproducible framework.

articles.title: Associations between socioeconomic status and time, risk, and social decision-making
articles.authors: Christian Benitez; Michelle Mahecha; Sarah K Garrison; Joe Kable
articles.abstract: Theories abound about the relationship between socioeconomic status (SES) and decision-making, but there is little empirical consensus on the nature and direction of any relationships between SES and time, risk, and social decision-making. These empirical inconsistencies may partly stem from difficulties in robustly capturing SES's multidimensionality and rigorously measuring time, risk, and social preferences. We aimed to clarify how SES affects decision-making by incorporating several objective and subjective measures of current and childhood SES, along with validated economic choice tasks that can accurately estimate individual preferences in a model-based manner, across three pre-registered studies (total N =3010) powered to detect small associations (i.e., |r| = 0.10). In Study 1, we found that SES is associated with time but not risk or social preferences. In Study 2, we found no associations between SES and preferences for risk or ambiguity. In Study 3, we found associations between SES and time preferences across monetary, health, and social rewards. Additionally, we discovered that different aspects of SES (education versus financial strain) influence time preferences via different psychological considerations (opportunity costs versus risks and pressing needs). Thus, we observe strong and specific links between SES and domain-general time preferences that can be traced to multiple psychological pathways but find little evidence for broader effects of SES on risky and social decisions.

articles.title: A Markov Chain Monte Carlo Procedure with Simulated Annealing for Shortening Assessments
articles.authors: Klint Kanopka; Xiaoxuan Zhang; Daphna Harel
articles.abstract: Symptom screeners, patient assessments, and cognitive tests are used to measure constructs such as health-related quality of life, levels of mental health symptoms, and ability. The items are often summarized, through a simple unweighted sum score or through more advanced methods, including factor analysis, Rasch modeling, and item response theory. However, long instruments increase respondent burden and it may be desirable to shorten them. One consideration that is often overlooked is that in many contexts, these instruments are used less for precise measurement of a latent construct but instead for accurate prediction of a diagnosis, expert judgment, or classification. As such, we present a method to shorten instruments that prioritizes reproduction of expert judgment based on an unweighted sum score through the use of a Markov Chain Monte Carlo algorithm with simulated annealing to explore the search space of potential short forms. This method is orders of magnitude more efficient than brute force search and has the advantage of optimizing for prediction of the desired outcome directly, instead of optimizing on the measurement of an intermediate outcome that may not be perfectly aligned with the decision criteria of interest. We prove the correctness of the algorithm, demonstrate its effectiveness and efficiency through simulation studies, and apply it to a screener for alcohol use disorder, demonstrating that under the unweighted sum score scoring constraint, shortened forms can more accurately predict expert judgments than full forms.

articles.title: Striking the Balance: How Variability Shapes Retrieval Practice and Worked Examples for Transfer Learning
articles.authors: Meng Cao; Paulo F. Carvalho
articles.abstract: The ability to generalize and apply knowledge to new situations is the hallmark of effective learning. There are multiple ways to improve learning towards generalization. Retrieval practice and worked examples have both been shown to improve learning, retention, and generalization. The literature on category learning also suggests that exposure to more varied examples, rather than the same example repeatedly, improves generalization. However, while retrieval practice is usually investigated with repeated examples, worked examples learning is usually investigated using varied content. Across two studies, we investigated how combining content variability with worked examples or retrieval practice promotes generalization when students receive initial instruction before practice (Experiment 1) and when they do not (Experiment 2). We found that when learners received initial instruction, retrieval practice led to better generalization compared to worked examples, regardless of whether the stimuli were repeated or varied. When initial instruction was not provided, worked examples resulted in better generalization than retrieval practice when items were repeated. However, retrieval practice produced comparable generalization to worked examples when items were varied. These findings suggest that variability supports generalization, particularly when learners must induce underlying rules, and that retrieval practice, traditionally associated with memory retention, can also facilitate inductive learning when applied to varied examples. This study emphasizes the importance of adapting learning strategies to align with the instructional context and task demands.

articles.title: Developing a psychological test battery to measure cognition in daily life
articles.authors: Andrew J. Aschenbrenner; Muchen Xi; Jeremy Cohn; Derek Alexander Simon; Joshua James Jackson
articles.abstract: Cognition is not a static process but is subject to substantial and meaningful variation within individuals over time. This has led to an increase in studies that aim to describe cognition in daily life by sampling participants repeatedly and remotely. Such studies, which we call high-frequency cognitive assessment or HFCA, tend to use a limited number of brief cognitive tests. This focus on a small number of tests leaves open questions concerning the psychometric characteristics of a wide array of cognitive tasks useful for HFCA that can guide researchers on appropriate task selection. We developed the Cognitive Variability Battery (CVB) a series of 9 cognitive tests clustered into three distinct cognitive domains, attentional control, processing speed, and episodic memory. CVB was administered to participants 3 times per day for 3 weeks. Cognitive tests were rotated to reduce the length of any single testing session. Each test was administered up to 60 times. We provide detailed descriptions of the performance of each task including variability, skew, and reliability statistics using intraclass correlations. We examine the sensitivity of each test to several contextual factors including stress, affect and social interactions. Finally, we provide power analyses on each cognitive test to determine how many assessments and participants are needed to detect effects of interest. These analyses will be essential to anyone seeking to implement HFCA testing in their own research programs by providing guidance on which cognitive tests to select and how many participants and observations may be needed.

articles.title: Are Redundant Labels Helpful during Category Learning? Replication of Lupyan, Rakison, & McClelland (2007)
articles.authors: Caroline Kaicher; Ellen Markman; Laura Gwilliams
articles.abstract: Labels are often advantageous for category learning. However, this may not be true for children as they learn subordinate, fine-grained object categories. In preparation for future research on subordinate category learning in children, we aimed to refine a procedure investigating the label advantage during subordinate category learning in adults. Thus, we conducted an online replication of Experiment 2 from Lupyan, Rakison, & McClelland (2007), which compares category learning under an auditory or written label condition versus a no label and non-linguistic cue condition in adults. We failed to replicate the key result, that those who received labels learned the categories faster.

articles.title: Belief in a Just World and Deception Detection Accuracy: Two Unsuccessful Conceptual Replications of Schindler and Reinhard (2015)
articles.authors: David Matthew Markowitz
articles.abstract: The current paper presents conceptual replication attempts of Schindler and Reinhard (2015), who found a negative association between belief in a just world (BJW) — the positive illusion that the world is a just and orderly place — and deception detection accuracy. Two studies, one with undergraduates (Study 1 N = 176) and the other with members of the general public (Study 2 N = 352), failed to obtain this negative relationship and instead found a null effect. Exploratory analyses observed that in the general public sample, BJW was positively associated with truth-bias (one’s tendency to ascribe a message as honest independent of its actual veracity) and both raw and signal detection theory metrics produced consistent results. In sum, individual differences like belief in a just world are faint and generally unrelated to deception detection accuracy at least in the current research settings, though future work would benefit from boundary condition testing.

articles.title: Understanding acceptability of a brief intervention for intrusive thoughts: A mixed- methods study
articles.authors: Megan Rose Serody; Clare Beatty; Erica Szkody; Jason Katz; Mary Kowalchyk; Aprajita Mohanty; Jessica L. Schleider
articles.abstract:
Intrusive thoughts (ITs) are common yet distressing experiences for many adolescents
and young adults. Brief, accessible interventions that provide psychoeducation and
coping strategies are needed, yet little is known about how youth perceive such
interventions. The present study examined both qualitative and quantitative indicators
of acceptability in adolescents and young adults with ITs who completed a digital
single-session intervention (SSI). The study aimed to gather intervention feedback and
determine whether symptom improvement and/or demographic factors predicted
acceptability. We conducted a secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial
comparing our SSI to an information-only control group in individuals aged 13-25,
focusing solely on acceptability factors of the SSI condition. Thematic analyses
revealed that young people with ITs prefer interventions that normalize the experience
of ITs, are emotionally validating, accessible and engaging, stimulate self-reflection,
and contain evidence-based strategies. Importantly, those who reported qualitatively
accepting the intervention were more likely to experience a reduction in hopelessness
symptoms at post-intervention; however, acceptability was not associated with distress
reduction. Of interest, those who identified as non-Caucasian were less likely to report
they had a positive intervention experience compared to Caucasian individuals.
Participants reported wanting more diverse examples of ITs to connect more with the
psychoeducation, as well as more personalized examples for validation. These findings
indicate acceptability factors and barriers that young adults with ITs experienced during
a brief SSI. Future studies, employing more diverse examples of ITs, will be crucial for
understanding further effects on acceptability and engagement.

articles.title: Computational mechanisms behind the vicious cycle of aversive beliefs and avoidance
articles.authors: Luianta Verra; Toby Wise; Ondrej Zika; Nicolas W Schuck
articles.abstract:
Heightened threat beliefs and avoidance are core symptoms of anxiety-related psychopathology. Yet, how beliefs drive avoidance and whether avoidance in turn prevents those beliefs from being updated remains unclear. Here, we tested this bidirectional relationship in N=236 participants using a gamified aversive learning and spatial navigation task in which participants rated threat before and after making costly avoidance decisions. We combined behavioural analyses with computational modelling to quantify threat generalisation, avoidance, and their association with transdiagnostic symptom dimensions derived from self-report measures.
Participants generalised threat to nearby locations, and higher location-specific threat beliefs predicted subsequent avoidance. In turn, avoidance shaped belief updating: visiting a location reduced threat beliefs even in the absence of location-specific feedback, whereas avoiding a location preserved them. We find that individuals scoring high on a transdiagnostic symptom dimension capturing worry and Intolerance of Uncertainty are overall more avoidant and that this behaviour was best explained by increased threat beliefs, not a general tendency to avoid more. 
In this work we identify a bidirectional link between beliefs and behaviour in aversive decision-making and show that maladaptive avoidance arises from how threat is inferred and generalized. These findings identify a self-reinforcing cycle, in which threat beliefs guide avoidance and avoidance prevents those beliefs from being revised, which may be a critical contributor to the maintenance of pathological anxiety.

articles.title: Children’s reasoning about possible outcomes of events in the present and the future
articles.authors: Esra Nur Küçük; Melissa M. Kibbe
articles.abstract: When making decisions, we often must consider multiple alternative outcomes of events that will happen in the future, or events that have already happened but the outcome is unknown. How do children navigate uncertainty across different points in time? Here, we tested several developmental hypotheses for children’s ability to reason about possibilities in the present and in the future. In two experiments (n = 192, US 3- and 4-year-olds), children were asked to prepare for two mutually exclusive possible outcomes of an event that either will occur in the future (Future condition) or that had already occurred but the outcome was currently unknown (Present condition). In Experiment 1 (n = 96), children were asked to reason about the possible location of an object in an event. In Experiment 2 (n = 96), children were asked to reason about the possible identity of an object in an event. In both experiments, we replicated previous patterns of success with future possibility reasoning, and found no differences in children’s ability to reason about possible outcomes in the present versus the future. Our results suggest that the ability to navigate uncertainty across different timepoints may emerge together in early development.

articles.title: From the Social to the Individual: Human Interdependence Calls for Explanatory Reversal in Mind and Brain
articles.authors: Haily Merritt; Dimitris Bolis
articles.abstract: Recent years have witnessed an “interactive turn” in the cognitive sciences, increasingly foregrounding real-time social interaction and organism–environment dynamics. Yet, even the interactive turn often stops short of its own implications, leaving an individual-first explanatory order largely intact. Taking these implications seriously points toward an explanatory reversal: human interdependence is primary, and individual cognition emerges within—and remains dependent upon—relational dynamics. In this review article, selectively drawing on philosophy of mind, developmental psychology, social neuroscience, neurophysiology and neuroecology, we set out a social-first perspective in which regulation, vulnerability, and agency are shaped through histories of affective, behavioral, and physiological co-regulation, including patterns of attunement, misattunement, and repair. Using interpersonal synchrony as a diagnostic case, we argue that recent work makes it increasingly feasible to treat synchrony not only as a statistical marker of coordination, but as an entry point for analysing interactional organization and its effects on individual cognition across causal, directional, and multiscale dimensions. In this regard, we synthesize findings indicating that interpersonal coordination can shape not only social outcomes but also apparently individual processes, motivating closer attention to social-to-individual pathways across scales. Making these commitments explicit supports an explanatory reversal that proceeds from the social to the individual, initiating a shift from documenting covariation to explaining how interaction reorganizes cognition across minds, bodies, and environments. We conclude by drawing out the implications of this shift for theory, empirical research, artificial intelligence, and societal practice.

articles.title: Targeting Emotion Coping Beliefs in Young Adults: A Randomized Controlled Trial of An Online Single-Session Intervention
articles.authors: Riley McDanal; Hein Kim; Pau Ortells-Faci; Nicholas R Eaton; Jessica L. Schleider
articles.abstract: Objective: Traditional psychological interventions pose various access barriers related to time, geography, provider availability, and cost. Online, self-directed single-session interventions (SSIs) can mitigate such access barriers. However, SSI effect sizes are highly heterogenous. Targeting core transdiagnostic processes may improve SSI consistency. This randomized controlled trial (registration NCT07034027 via clinicaltrials.gov) evaluated a novel SSI targeting the transdiagnostic construct of emotion coping beliefs. Method: Participants aged 18-29 reporting deficits in emotional nonreactivity abilities (N = 550) were randomized to the SSI condition or the control and completed questionnaires assessing emotion intolerance beliefs and related constructs at baseline, post-activity, and two-week follow-up. Multilevel modeling was used to assess differential proximal and distal change in emotion intolerance beliefs. Participants in the SSI condition also provided ratings of perceived intervention teaching effectiveness. Results: The first hypothesis — that proximal reductions in emotional intolerance beliefs would be greater for SSI participants than for control participants — was supported at p < 0.001, with a near-medium Cohen’s d effect size derived from a standard regression. This differential benefit was not sustained at follow-up. The second hypothesis — that participants would self-report the activity “somewhat” helped them learn to be nonreactive — was not clearly supported, as the mean rating was slightly below the preregistered cutoff. However, participants provided positive views of the intervention. Conclusions: The SSI appears promising as a highly scalable on-demand tool for acute distress accompanied by low coping self-efficacy. Future efforts should examine the conditions under which acute benefits might be sustained.

articles.title: Confidence phenotypes: a unified computational account of value and decision certainty in reinforcement learning
articles.authors: Nicolás A. Comay; Guillermo Solovey; Pablo Barttfeld
articles.abstract: Confidence, the “feeling of knowing” that accompanies every cognitive process, plays a critical role in human reinforcement learning; yet its computational bases in learning scenarios have only recently begun to be studied. Prior work has distinguished between value confidence (certainty in value estimates) and decision confidence (certainty that a choice is correct), but how these two forms of confidence are computed and interact has not been directly tested. Here we combine two experiments and previously published datasets to test competing computational hypotheses. We find that value confidence is best explained by a Bayesian computation reflecting the precision of value estimates, and that it adaptively guides behaviour by reducing exploration and promoting exploitation as certainty increases. In contrast, decision confidence departs from Bayesian predictions, especially on errors. A hybrid model integrating Bayesian probability with the precision of the decision variable better accounts for decision confidence. Moreover, the relative weights assigned to these two sources of information characterize individual differences in confidence reporting and, in addition, they are predictive of task and metacognitive performance, where the more Bayesian the subject, the higher the performance. Together, these results provide a unified computational mechanism through which distinct forms of confidence shape learning and choices in uncertain environments.

articles.title: A Framework for Evidence-Based Psychotherapy with AI (EBP-AI)
articles.authors: Elizabeth Cameron Stade; Philip Held; H. Andrew Schwartz; Shannon Wiltsey Stirman; johannes Christopher Eichstaedt
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems and large language models (LLMs) offer substantial potential to augment or even fundamentally change elements of psychological assessment and treatment. However, current AI technologies have yet to demonstrate the capacity to effect meaningful and sustained clinical change. This gap reflects both the limited integration of clinical science knowledge into language models and applications built using them, as well as the mismatch between the brief, minutes-long nature of most AI interactions and the months-long course of most evidence-based treatments. Here we introduce the Evidence-Based Psychotherapy with AI (EBP-AI) framework, which articulates a set of principles for developing effective clinical AI applications: 1) psychodiagnostic assessment, 2) longitudinal case conceptualization, 3) appropriately dosed intervention planning, 4) meaningful progress evaluation, 5) rigorous validation with clinical populations, 6) attention to real world implementation and use, 7) clinically appropriate style, and 8) understanding clinical psychology as a living science. We introduce a set of key technical questions for the development and evaluation of clinical LLMs and AIs aligned with these principles. Despite their potential, current clinical AIs fall short, in part due to issues with memory, sycophancy, and prioritizing short-term helpfulness over long-term clinical impact. Responsible and ethical design of effective, clinical-science-based AI systems will require understanding their limitations and strategically extending their capabilities.

articles.title: Inoculating against microtargeted political advertising: A personal values perspective
articles.authors: Charlotte Brown; Almog Simchon; Stephan Lewandowsky
articles.abstract: Political microtargeting, the tailoring of political messages to individuals based on their personal characteristics, poses risks to democratic decision-making. Previous research has shown that prompting individuals to reflect on their personality traits can enhance recognition of personality-based targeting. The present studies extend this approach to personal values, which are stronger predictors of political orientation and voting behaviour. Across three experiments with 1,247 British participants, we tested whether boosting interventions that encourage reflection on value priorities improve recognition of value-based political microtargeting. Using validated real-world political advertisements, we found that boosting increased participants’ ability to identify when ads matched their values and sharpened their sensitivity to mismatches. Although results varied across individual studies, internal meta-analyses confirmed significant overall effects for both recognition of matched ads and discrimination of mismatched ads. These findings provide the first empirical evidence that value-based boosting interventions can strengthen recognition of political microtargeting.

articles.title: Digital shadows in mental health map how LLMs simulate depression, anxiety, and stress through language and psychometrics
articles.authors: Emma Franchino; Rodolfo Rizzi; Edoardo Sebastiano De Duro; Massimo Stella
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for mental-health support, yet few datasets characterise how prompting conditions shape LLMs' psychological and linguistic outputs. To fill this gap, we introduce Mental Health Digital Shadows (MHDS), a benchmark of 75,000 outputs from 15 LLMs in conversation, under human-shadow or LLM-assistant conditions. MHDS includes outputs generated by several model families (Grok, DeepSeek, Mistral, and Qwen) or providers (Microsoft, IBM, and OpenAI). Human shadows are conditioned on sociodemographic and personality traits alongside assessments of their depression, anxiety, and stress levels, based on the Depression Anxiety and Stress Scale-21 (DASS-21). MHDS incorporates DASS-21 psychometric scores and three types of natural language texts: (i) an item-by-item explanation of shadows' DASS-21 numerical scores; (ii) a list of words expressing feelings; and (iii) six topic-oriented open-ended replies addressing mental health topics (e.g., stigma, support, and AI psychologists). Validating MHDS across its two conditions identifies a tendency for human shadows to alter their linguistic replies and psychometric scores according to prompting conditions, a pattern reminiscent of human behaviour and absent in LLM assistants. Further validation against human data finds that LLMs can produce distress scores resembling the distribution of human populations. Hence, MHDS offers a psychometrically grounded resource for studying how LLMs can debate mental health conditions, with applications in NLP, AI safety, bias detection, and well-being AI support tools.

articles.title: Modelling the acquisition of spelling
articles.authors: Kristian Berg; Motoki Saito
articles.abstract:
Learning to spell German words can be hard. Beginning spellers often produce forms that
deviate from orthographically correct ones in various ways. In this paper, we demonstrate
that the proclivity to misspell a given word can be estimated using the Discriminative Lex-
icon Model (DLM, Baayen et al. 2019). DLM maps form representations onto meaning
representations using simple linear networks, and, in the case of written language, also
phonemic representations onto graphemic representations. We train three different DLM
models: one relates single phonemes to single graphemes; one relates phoneme bigrams to
grapheme bigrams, and one relates phoneme trigrams to grapheme trigrams. As a test case,
we use a longitudinal corpus of picture story descriptions of primary school children, LitKey
(Laarmann-Quante et al., 2019). We let the three models predict words that children actually
spelled (or misspelled), and we use Generalized Additive mixed-effect models to determine
the alignment, and the influence that factors like age and proficiency have on the alignment.
Our results suggest that context becomes more important as children become more profi-
cient. Beginning spellers can best be approximated with the unigram model; intermediate
spellers are best approximated by the bigram model; and children at the end of primary school
are best approximated by the trigram model. Even though we only take phonological forms
into account, the trigram model successfully captures spellings that are traditionally described
as morphological. We interpret our results as an argument in favor of statistical learning and
the increasing influence of grain size over time for developing spellers.

articles.title: A stakeholder-informed approach to the development and implementation of national digital registry for psychosis: findings from the EPICare platform
articles.authors: Rachel Upthegrove; Melanie Lafanechere; Megan A. Pope; India Francis-Crossley; John Ainsworth; Sophie Allan; Tumelo Bogatsu; Niyah Campbell; Richard Drake; Sonia Johnson
articles.abstract:
Introduction
Early Intervention in Psychosis (EIP) services reduce the burden of psychotic disorders, yet significant inequities persist across England in access to NICE‑recommended treatments, provision of psychological therapies, and long‑term outcomes. These disparities are compounded by fragmented electronic health record (EHR) systems, inconsistent data capture, and limited interoperability, preventing routine real‑time evaluation of care quality and effectiveness. The Early Psychosis Informatics into Care (EPICare) programme aims to develop a national learning healthcare system incorporating a standardised core dataset and clinician dashboard to support equitable, data‑driven psychosis care. 

Methods
Our stakeholder co-design approach comprised three interlinked work packages delivered between 2021 and 2022. Using a participatory co‑design approach, four multi‑stakeholder workshops and preparatory lived‑experience sessions were conducted to identify essential data elements, refine content, and develop dashboard requirements. Stakeholders included people with lived experience, carers, clinicians, informaticians, academics, and policymakers. Parallel informatics work examined EHR systems across five NHS Mental Health Trusts to assess technical feasibility, data maturity, and harmonisation requirements. Implementation research—via interviews and workshop observations—evaluated anticipated barriers and facilitators to future adoption. 

Results
Consensus was achieved on a core EPICare dataset including sociodemographic variables, NICE psychosis standard measures, PROMs (DIALOG, GBO, ReQOL), CROMs (HoNOS, symptoms), and longitudinal symptom data. Stakeholders defined key dashboard features such as outcome notifications, treatment reminders, and performance indicators, prioritising usability and alignment with clinical workflow. Substantial variation in Trust‑level data infrastructure informed a flexible technical architecture to mitigate implementation risk. 

Discussion
Our EPICare stakeholder engagement delivered a co‑designed, technically feasible and nationally scalable specification for a psychosis learning health system, enabling progression to platform development and piloting across five NHS demonstrator Trusts.

articles.title: Limited evidence that reputation-based partner choice facilitates information sharing in humans
articles.authors: Angel V Jimenez; Lei Chang; Keith Jensen; Hui Jing Lu; Alex Mesoudi
articles.abstract: A necessary prerequisite for the accumulation of beneficial knowledge, or ‘cumulative cultural evolution’, is the sharing of information via social learning. Yet little work in the field of cultural evolution has examined the mechanisms that support information sharing in the face of exploitative information free-riding and information hoarding. We ran a series of online interactive experiments (N=716) combined with computational reinforcement and social learning models to test whether the mechanism of reputation-based partner choice can effectively support information sharing. Participants in groups chose whether to (i) engage in costly innovation and (ii) whether to share the resultant knowledge. Sharers received increased reputations for sharing and participants could use reputations to select recipients of knowledge. We found strong priors for information sharing that persisted throughout the experiments in participants from both the UK and China (Study 1). However, partner choice was generally too weak to explain the presence of widespread information sharing, which persisted even when we reduced the benefit of innovation (Study 2), introduced exploitative bots (Study 3), and removed reputations altogether (Study 4). Our results suggest that indiscriminate, group-based sharing is more important for facilitating cumulative cultural evolution than discriminate, reputation-based sharing.

articles.title: From Green to Technology:  Age Group Variability in Conceptual Complexity and Concreteness
articles.authors: Ilenia Falcinelli; Chiara Fini; Claudia Mazzuca; Anna M. Borghi
articles.abstract:
Concepts are widely recognized as flexible and shaped by multiple factors. Here, we investigate how conceptual representations differ across age groups by focusing on four timely domains—ecological, geo, natural (which we labeled altogether green), and technological. Replicating a previous study, we collected ratings from 320 Italian older adults (aged >64) on 50 concepts per domain across 39 semantic dimensions targeting different conceptual aspects (e.g., sensorimotor, sociopolitical, inner, emotional). We then compared these ratings with those from younger adults (aged 18–35) collected in the earlier study.
Results revealed strong semantic proximity among green concepts and a clear separation between them (especially natural) and the technological ones. The internal semantic composition of each domain revealed clear age group differences. Within the green domains, older adults exhibited richer, more multifaceted, and more concrete conceptual representations than younger adults, with fine-grained divergences across green concepts. In contrast, no age group differences emerged in the complexity of technological concept representations; however, younger adults characterized this domain more concretely.
These differences were closely linked to self-reported domain experience and familiarity: older adults reported greater personal experience with ecological topics, whereas younger adults reported higher familiarity with technology.
Theoretically, our findings suggest that conceptual complexity and concreteness vary across domains and are shaped by age group membership and all that it entails. Scientifically, the resulting O-TECo database provides the first semantic norms tailored to older adults. Societally, results highlight the need for age-sensitive interventions to foster engagement with environmental policies and responsible technology use.

articles.title: A Call to Action: Open Assessment Measures in Developmental Psychology
articles.authors: Marta Topor; Henrik Danielsson
articles.abstract:
Cognitive and behavioural development research often relies on standardised and validated tests, which can indicate whether performance falls within typical or atypical boundaries for a given chronological age. The choice of a test in a research project is not straightforward as most of these tests are owned by publishing companies who limit access and research use of these tests. We present an overview of the most commonly used tests for assessing reasoning, general cognitive ability and behavioural functioning in children, adolescents and young adults. We focus on normed cognitive and behavioural measures, which facilitate the identification of specific difficulties relevant to research on atypical cognitive development within the fields of psychology and education. We discuss the consequences of the limited access to the standardised measures based on a case example where barriers to access significantly limited research design for a big team science project on intellectual disability. We outline a plan for collective action to establish public domain assessment tools. In the interest of scientific progression and international collaboration, research measures should not be subject to restrictive copyright laws.

Corresponding author Marta Topor: marta.topor@liu.se

articles.title: Egostasis: A First-Principles Account of the Neurocomputational Basis of Suffering
articles.authors: James Cooke
articles.abstract: This paper introduces the concept of egostasis (self-based goal-directed persistence behaviour) and proposes a novel neurophenomenological theory of the experience of suffering, grounded in the framework of active inference. We argue that psychological suffering is a transmodal, aversive state arising from prediction errors being attributed to a self-model. Chronic suffering is proposed to arise from temporally and counterfactually deep action policies that prevent the resolution of interoceptive stress signalling. While homeostasis and allostasis resolve stressors through immediate physiological and behavioural adjustments, egostasis attempts to achieve goals over extended temporal horizons. We hypothesise that chronic suffering arises when this mode becomes entrenched via canalisation, perpetuating a dynamic in which temporally deep egostatic policies (mediated by the Default Mode and Central Executive Networks) consistently override temporally shallow allostatic policies. This results in a “selfing” feedback loop: maintained interoceptive stress signals recruit the self-model (egostasis) which perpetuates the very stress-signalling the system seeks to resolve. The egostasis model provides a transdiagnostic framework for understanding psychiatric disorders, including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and addiction, as facets of a systems-level failure in psychological flexibility that perpetuates the prediction errors produced under egostasis. By identifying the recruitment of the self-model as the core mechanism of chronic suffering, this theory elucidates the therapeutic efficacy of interventions that disrupt “selfing” dynamics, such as mindfulness meditation and psychedelic-induced “ego-dissolution”, offering a path toward the relief of suffering by restoring adaptive allostasis.

articles.title: Decision Outcomes for Ill-Structured Problems in Top Management Teams: mapping Facilitators and Barriers in a Scoping Review
articles.authors: Johan Cornelis van Donkelaar; Maitta Spronken; J. Van Ruysseveldt; Madelon van Hooff
articles.abstract: Top Management Teams (TMTs) of many organizations struggle with ill-structured problems due to volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous environments. Our objective was to obtain insight into facilitators and barriers contributing to decisions in ill-structured problem settings. A scoping review following the Prisma-SCR protocol was conducted, resulting in 39 included studies. The most investigated decision outcomes were decision quality, decision commitment and decision effectiveness. Facilitators were (1) behavioral integration; (2) procedural rationality; (3) task conflict and (4) trust. Relationship conflict was a barrier. Political behavior, (team) leadership and diversity can either be a facilitator, a barrier or a secondary predictor. Perhaps most surprisingly is what we did not find: the role of emotions during decision making. Affect is underexposed in empirical research about team decision-making. Our findings suggest that decision outcomes are shaped by a complex interaction between primary and secondary predictors. Future research may expose how barriers and facilitators jointly affect the various types of decision outcomes, also over time. Moreover, we recommend more attention for behavioral integration and conflict mitigation. Lastly, the role of emotions should be incorporated within research designs about top level decision-making.

articles.title: Brass-for-Brains: Analog Computation for Cognition
articles.authors: Jannis Friedrich
articles.abstract: Analog computation is often portrayed as an obscure historical relic, while its counterpart, digital computation, flavours much cognitive science. Yet, this is a mischaracterization which undermines analog computation’s unique strengths. Digital computation’s defining features, step-wise algorithmic processing based on arbitrary representations, are precisely what make it unsuited for the types of target systems which confront cognition. Analog computation, on the other hand, models the causal structure of target systems, and by exploiting physical mechanics, is naturally and efficiently suited to this challenge. Yet, this fact has gone unnoticed in contemporary literature addressing how (or if) cognition computes. This paper repackages recent advances in theorizing on analog computation directly for empirical work, linking it to findings and theories in cognitive science. To do this, it draws on brass-for-brains, a case example from early tide predicting machines, which depicts how physical mechanics perform tasks which are laborious and challenging for digital computation. This uncovers unjustified assumptions around central explanatory constructs such as the anatomical limits of computation, the distinction of representation and computation, and the distinctiveness of cognition from other physical phenomena. We then outline the considerable evidence and theories in cognitive science already implementing analog computation’s principles of resemblance representations and computation by physical mechanics. Brass-for-brains re-presents analog computation as not only empirically supported, but as a necessary meta-theoretical category. It orders emerging paradigms and uncovers productive underlying theoretical boundaries in current research.

articles.title: The Impact of Mood and Affective Priming on Art Evaluation:  A Systematic Literature Review
articles.authors: Corinna Kühnapfel; Julian Kutsche; Joerg Fingerhut
articles.abstract:
Many studies in empirical aesthetics investigate emotional responses to art. Yet the role of affective states that precede an encounter with art remains surprisingly underexamined. This systematic review synthesizes evidence on how affective priming, experimentally induced mood, as well as baseline mood shape the evaluation of visual art. Following PRISMA guidelines, we searched PsycINFO, Scopus, and PubMed (1970–2025), yielding 68 records. Eight studies met inclusion criteria, and seven additional studies were identified via backward citation tracking and supplementary searches, resulting in 15 quantitative studies (N = 1,600).
Across studies, affective influences were categorized as affect-congruent, affect-incongruent, arousal-related, or context-dependent. Overall, preceding affect can modulate core evaluative outcomes such as liking, beauty, interest, and sublimity, but effects are heterogeneous. Several priming paradigms show affect-congruent shifts in liking, whereas other studies report affect-incongruent enhancements (e.g., fear increasing sublimity or negative primes increasing beauty). Induced mood findings further suggest sensitivity to the specific induction method (e.g., clearer evaluation shifts for text-based, self-focused inductions than for film clips). Baseline mood effects appear more conditional and context-dependent, emerging most clearly when contextual information or task demands invite open elaboration. 
We map the evidence onto Forgas’s Affect Infusion Model (AIM), arguing that variability across findings is interpretable in terms of judgment strategies (heuristic vs. substantive vs. motivated processing) and corresponding routes of affect integration. We conclude with methodological priorities and future directions aimed at directly testing mechanisms and embodied pathways through which affect shapes aesthetic evaluation.

articles.title: The wurst case scenario: A pictorial intervention to change the appeal of meat-based dishes.
articles.authors: Jonas Potthoff; Maximilian Gerschütz; Anne Schienle; Maya Gumussoy
articles.abstract:
Background: Overconsumption of meat negatively impacts both the environment and human health, yet global meat consumption continues to rise. Therefore, effective and easily implementable interventions are needed to encourage reductions in meat consumption. This study builds on prior research suggesting that exposure to depictions of farm animals in positive, free-range, natural settings may reduce the desire to eat meat.
Methods: Participants completed a visual search task requiring them to distinguish between images of meat-based and vegan dishes. Before this task, participants were randomly assigned to two groups and exposed to images of either cows/pigs (intervention) or cats/dogs (control group). Self-reported desire to eat meat, reaction times and response errors were recorded for images of meat-based dishes.
Results: Exposure to images of cows and pigs reduced visual attention to meat-based dishes more than exposure to cats and dogs. The desire to eat meat was not reduced. On the contrary, there was a trend in the opposite direction with an increase in meat appetite.
Discussion: Participants may have perceived the animals as symbols of cruelty-free farming practices, reducing ethical concerns and potentially justifying meat consumption.

articles.title: Testing Potential Mechanisms of the Description-Experience Gap in Loss Aversion
articles.authors: Nuno Busch; Thorsten Pachur
articles.abstract: Recent research has found a novel description-experience gap in loss aversion, such that the overweighting of losses relative to gains in risky choice is more pronounced when people make decisions based on experienced rather than on descriptive summary information. Here, we tested two possible mechanisms of this gap: a decision-by-sampling (DbS) account, according to which the relative distributions of gain and loss outcomes that the decision maker encounters differ between description and experience; and an optional stopping account, according to which the role of losses for stopping information search differs between description and experience. Neither of the two mechanisms explained the gap. Whereas in description the relative rank of losses was higher than that of gains, this pattern was not more pronounced in experience (in fact, it was even reversed)—at odds with a DbS account. Further, although individual differences in the tendency of search termination after encountering a loss (vs. a gain) showed some association with individual-level loss aversion, this mechanism did not mediate the description-experience gap in loss aversion. Our results clarify that two prominent mechanisms of preference construction are unlikely to contribute to the description-experience gap in loss aversion, clearing the view on potential contributions of alternative mechanisms (e.g., asymmetric learning of gains and losses).

articles.title: New Voices for Environmental Causes – The Psychological Basis of Different Reactions to Posts by Human and Virtual Greenfluencers
articles.authors: Nils Burgheim; Cornelia Sindermann
articles.abstract:
Despite the growing presence of Virtual Influencers (VIs) and their potentially far-reaching impacts, their socio-political effects remain underexplored. The present paper provides the first systematic investigation of audience reactions to posts by human versus virtual greenfluencers. Drawing on persuasion research and Social Identity Theory, we hypothesized that posts by human versus virtual influencers and gender-congruent influencers are rated as more credible and that these variables may also be linked to engagement. Furthermore, we expected that trust in humans and AI are positively related to the perceived credibility of posts of the respective influencer type. We examined relations with engagement exploratorily.
In a 3×2-mixed online experiment, N = 410 participants were presented either with posts by (i) AI-controlled virtual greenfluencers, (ii) human-controlled virtual greenfluencers, or (iii) human greenfluencers. Each participant further saw posts by a greenfluencer depicting (i) a man and (ii) a woman. Participants self-reported perceived credibility and engagement intentions with each post. 
Results show that while gender congruency had no significant effects, participants rated human greenfluencer posts as most credible and showed higher engagement with them compared to posts by both virtual greenfluencer types. Trust in AI emerged as a relevant but nuanced predictor of the different outcomes for different greenfluencer types.
As virtual influencers become more prevalent, differences in reactions to them versus human influencers may attenuate. Given their scalability, it thus remains important to examine when and for whom virtual political influencers and their posts may elicit comparable or even stronger reactions than human influencers.

articles.title: Computer Vision and Machine Learning Reveal Developmental Changes in Continuous Motor Planning
articles.authors: Ori Ossmy; Ajay Krishna; Tommaso Ghilardi; Juliette Pope; Sinisa Todorovic; Karen E Adolph
articles.abstract: Perception and action comprise a continual loop, where the just-prior action generates perceptual information for planning the upcoming action. Building a block tower requires updates to motor planning in a chain of perception-action loops. Each new block requires updates in the trajectory of the acting hand to place the block and updates in the position of the supporting hand to hold the tower steady. Eight-six participants (four-, six-, and eight-year-old children and adults, n = 20-23 per age group) built towers in a stable, “flush” condition (all blocks aligned) and an unstable, “offset” condition (initial block cantilevered). Head-mounted eye tracking recorded point of gaze; computer vision tracked hand and block movements; and machine learning tested whether participants used perceptual information about tower height and stability to update their motor plans. With age, children built higher towers with fewer breaks and shifted gaze earlier to the new block. Eight-year-olds and adults systematically changed the position of their supporting hand based on tower height and whether the condition was flush or offset, but younger children did not. Importantly, the spatiotemporal patterns of movements in both acting and supporting hands were similar for four- and six-year-olds and for eight-year-olds and adults, but differed between younger and older age groups. Findings show that children learn to update continuous motor plans using perceptual information about changes in the environment caused by their own just-prior actions. Moreover, findings illustrate the benefits of computer vision and machine learning to reveal developmental changes in motor planning.

articles.title: Social Capital, Socioeconomic Status, and Depression and Anxiety Among Young People in Latin America: A Case-Control Study
articles.authors: Hannah Gray; Georgie Hudson; Diliniya Stanislaus Sureshkumar; Catherine Fung; Luis Brusco; Miguel Uribe; Carlos Gomez Restrepo; Francisco Diez-Canseco; Stefan Priebe; Victoria J Bird
articles.abstract:
Introduction. Growing evidence suggests that social capital is protective against common mental disorders, but how this affects adolescents and young adults living in highly deprived contexts outside of the Global North has received less attention. We investigated whether cognitive (i.e. trust, values) and structural (i.e. networks, support) social capital were associated with depression and anxiety in young people growing up in the poorest half of three cities in South America.

Methods. In this case-control study, we recruited young people, 15-16 and 20-24 years old, from the most deprived half of Bogotá (Colombia), Lima (Peru) and Buenos Aires (Argentina), who did and did not meet threshold criteria for depression and/or anxiety. Following multiple imputation for missing data, we used multivariable logistic regression to test whether cognitive and structural social capital were associated with case-control status, and we examined whether these were modified by socioeconomic status (SES).

Results.
