States have repeatedly sought to tether FET to the minimum standard, most notably the United States, a principal architect of the investment regime and a major capital exporter. Yet arbitral jurisprudence has tended toward broader interpretations. This Article asks why. It argues that the shift cannot be explained by doctrine, state power, credible commitment, firm pressure, or arbitrator discretion alone. The better explanation is jurisprudential drift: interpretive movement produced within a specialized field of arbitrators, elite counsel, arbitral institutions, and commentators. Open-textured treaty language and arbitral autonomy create interpretive space; investor demand supplies background pressure for capacious protection; and legal counsel and arbitrators benefit, generally but unevenly, from a more complex and protective legal regime. Symbolic capital helps make some broader interpretations focal; reputational enforcement keeps arbitrators and counsel aligned with those interpretations; and secrecy, judicial economy, and peer review supply flexibility, allowing tribunals to manage difficult cases without turning exceptions into a new doctrinal baseline. The Article develops this theory, compares it with existing and reconstructed accounts from law, international political economy, and the social sciences, and evaluates it against the historical, doctrinal, and arbitral record surrounding the rise of the investment regime, the minimum standard, and FET. It concludes that the drift toward autonomous FET reflects a form of transnational legal authority generated through ordinary legal reasoning and coordination within the field of investment arbitration.

articles.title: Mirror or Membrane? Semantic Information, Collateral, and the Limits of Green Finance
articles.authors: alessandro maresca
articles.abstract: Sustainable finance is usually framed as an information problem: if markets receive better information about climate and biodiversity, prices and allocation will adjust. This paper asks a different question: whether such information becomes *semantic* for finance — causally consequential for a specified viability. Drawing on Rovelli and Kolchinsky–Wolpert, I distinguish two viability functions for wholesale finance: financial viability ($V_1$), defined by settlement, liquidity and collateral acceptability; and biophysically conditioned viability ($V_2$), defined by the long-run persistence of the finance–economy system under planetary constraints. I apply the diagnostic to the collateral complex, focusing on Eurosystem collateral eligibility and euro-area repo markets. The argument turns on a permutation criterion: if two instruments differ biophysically but agree in the parameters governing eligibility, haircutting, substitution, rehypothecation and liquidation, the complex treats them identically, and biophysical information can be scrambled at no operative cost. The collateral complex is semantically rich under $V_1$ and approaches semantic emptiness under $V_2$ on the biophysical dimension as an independent operative variable. Green labels and taxonomies increase syntactic information; absent operative changes they cannot repair this semantic misalignment — an architecture densely responsive to the conditions of its own reproduction and structurally insensitive to the conditions of planetary reproduction.

articles.title: Book Chapter: Pharmaceutical Pricing
articles.authors: Qiang Liu; Siyi Yu; Siqi Wen; Yong Cai
articles.abstract:
This chapter examines pharmaceutical pricing through a five-part framework: Cost, Customers, Channels, Competitors, and Compatibility. We synthesize how research and development (R&D) risk, patent and exclusivity rules, insurer design, healthcare providers, patients, and intermediaries including pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs), wholesalers, pharmacies, and group purchasing organizations (GPOs), jointly shape list and net prices. The chapter distinguishes small-molecule and biologic cost structures; explains how patents, generics, and biosimilars alter competitive conduct; and shows how formularies, cost-sharing, copay programs, and patient-assistance mechanisms reallocate spending. We also integrate recent policy interventions, including Medicare Part D reforms, transparency mandates, drug-importation rules, and the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) negotiation authority, and discuss their implications for innovation incentives and patient access.

The chapter concludes with a forward-looking research agenda focused on developing innovative pricing models for high-cost therapies; examining global benchmarking and trade policies that influence R&D investment and affordability; assessing the effects of patient-assistance programs, copay coupons, and digital discount platforms; analyzing intermediary behavior and biosimilar competition within evolving market structures; and evaluating the impact of new U.S. pricing regulations through causal policy analysis.

articles.title: Unintended Politics: Opinion Expression and Incivility in Incidental Political Discussion
articles.authors: Yibin Fan; Benjamin Mako Hill; Patricia Moy
articles.abstract: While substantial research has focused on how political talk can reinforce or undermine democratic norms, relatively little attention has been paid to the role of incidental political discussion in everyday, nonpolitical contexts. Embedded within contexts of shared, nonpartisan identities or preexisting social relationships, existing theory suggests that incidental political discussion will include less political opinion and uncivil expression than purposive political discussion. Analyses of nearly three thousand comments on mass shootings and gun control across both political and nonpolitical communities on Reddit show that, contrary to expectations, incidental political discussions are more likely than purposive political discussions to contain expressions of policy opinion. Additionally, the level of incivility does not differ between the two kinds of discussions.

articles.title: Do ICE Arrests Spark Political Action?
articles.authors: Hans Hassell; Taylor Hill; John B. Holbein
articles.abstract: Does U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity trigger electoral mobilization? Theory and prior evidence suggest it should: historical enforcement has driven citizens to the polls, contemporary enforcement has sparked widespread protests, and detention and deportation impose exceptionally high stakes on affected communities. The second Trump administration’s enforcement surge represents perhaps the most theoretically favorable conditions ever observed for detecting such a response. Using data from 2023 to 2025, we test whether recent ICE activity has translated into changes in voter registration and political donations using a nationwide county × day panel. Using Difference-in-differences, event study, and regression discontinuity in time designs, we find largely null or near-null estimates. For voter registration, modest effects are confined to certain minority groups and counties with high concentrations of Democratic voters, foreign-born residents, or Hispanic populations, while for other groups and places, effects are statistically indistinguishable from zero. For donations, we detect substantively negligible effects—equivalent to roughly one additional donor contributing ≈$200 per county, present only on the day of arrest. Moreover, these effects are offsetting, positive for both Democrats and Republicans, and somewhat sensitive to violations of parallel trends. Our findings hold across a battery of explanations for the null and survive a demanding critical-case test using ICE activity in Minnesota in late 2025 and early 2026. Even where ICE activity generated substantial attention, effects are indistinguishable from zero. Together, our results suggest that even historically unprecedented immigration enforcement, in even the most salient local contexts, does not automatically translate into political mobilization. These findings contribute to the contested literature on the political consequences of the state’s exercise of punitive power.

articles.title: Marcos teóricos para democratizar el conocimiento científico y sus implicaciones en la salud pública
articles.authors: Luis Fernando Gómez
articles.abstract: Este ensayo ofrece un panorama de los principales abordajes teóricos orientados a la democratización de la ciencia: a) la nueva producción del conocimiento, b) la ciencia posnormal y c) la ciencia ciudadana; asimismo, se analizan las implicaciones de estos enfoques para la salud pública. En todos ellos se observa un reconocimiento explícito de la incertidumbre, con un mayor énfasis y desarrollo teórico en la ciencia posnormal. Los tres enfoques promueven procesos de participación que permiten a la ciudadanía comprender tanto las fortalezas como las limitaciones del conocimiento científico. En el caso de la ciencia ciudadana, se identifican posturas diversas que abogan por un mayor grado de participación e involucramiento de la ciudadanía en la toma de decisiones. La ciencia posnormal enfatiza que, pese a la incertidumbre, existen circunstancias en las que resulta urgente implementar acciones debido a lo que está en juego; este enfoque teórico reconoce, además, la relevancia del conocimiento no científico y de una comunidad ampliada de revisores para legitimar la implementación de intervenciones. Por su parte, la nueva producción del conocimiento plantea la necesidad de interactuar con la sociedad privilegiando el conocimiento científico, lo que supone un desafío de legitimidad en el ámbito de la salud pública, debido a que podría favorecer una participación inequitativa, a expensas de los sectores ciudadanos con menores niveles educativos.

articles.title: THE DISPLACEMENT HYPOTHESIS Does Suppression of Consensual Adult Sexual Activity Correlate with Increased Child Sexual Victimisation? A Comparative Empirical Analysis
articles.authors: Aditya Gupta
articles.abstract:
Child sexual abuse remains among the most egregious manifestations of predatory human behaviour, yet its structural determinants are seldom examined through the lens of regulatory failure rather than individual pathology alone. This paper advances the Displacement Hypothesis: when legitimate, consensual, and regulated expressions of adult sexuality are systematically suppressed — through criminalisation, social stigma, or institutional prohibition — the resulting demand doesn’t extinguish itself; rather, it displaces toward victims who are most structurally vulnerable, including children and non-consenting adults.

Drawing on comparative empirical data from six jurisdictions — the Netherlands, New Zealand, Rhode Island (USA), Sweden, Germany, and India — this paper examines documented correlations between the regulatory architecture governing consensual adult sexual activity and the incidence of child sexual victimisation and coercive sexual crime. The analysis is descriptive and comparative; it makes no normative prescription regarding any particular regulatory model, but identifies a significant research gap warranting systematic empirical investigation in the Indian context.

The paper integrates three significant bodies of contemporary evidence: the global #MeToo movement (2017–present) as a structural indicator of the pervasive institutional tolerance of sexual harm; the Supreme Court of India's progressive jurisprudence on sex worker dignity in Buddhadev Karmaskar v. State of West Bengal (2011, 2022); and the comparative regulatory evidence from decriminalised jurisdictions. The paper situates this analysis within the broader Regulatory Realism framework developed across the author's prior published works, arguing that any comprehensive primary prevention strategy for child sexual abuse — including the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model proposed in Gupta (2026a) — must account for the structural demand-side dynamics that current Indian law leaves unaddressed.

articles.title: Early AI Adoption and Firm Productivity Growth in a Middle-Income Economy: Evidence from Colombia
articles.authors: Juan Duran-Vanegas
articles.abstract: This paper examines the relationship between artificial intelligence (AI) adoption and firm-level productivity growth in a middle-income economy. Combining data on AI use from the 2019 Colombian Enterprise ICT Survey with longitudinal manufacturing data, I estimate productivity growth differentials between adopters and non-adopters while accounting for pre-adoption characteristics and productivity trajectories using entropy balancing.   AI adoption is associated with a 16 percent cumulative increase in labor productivity over 2016–2019, equivalent to roughly 5 percent annualized growth. These differentials appear to be driven by higher sales and value added rather than reductions in costs or employment, are similar among in-house and outsourced AI developments, and increase for firms with higher pre-existing technical capabilities. Finally, the analysis points to changes in organizational structure as a potential adjustment margin. AI adoption is associated with a small but significant decline in the share of administrative workers, suggesting a reallocation of tasks away from administrative functions.

articles.title: Buzz as an Urban Resource
articles.authors: Daniel Silver; Terry Nichols Clark
articles.abstract: The global rise of arts and culture is transforming local politics. Though new to many academic urban analysts, this is a commonplace for many mayors and local policy makers around the world. We seek to overcome this divide by joining culture and the arts with classic concepts of urban politics. We offer an analytical framework incorporating the politics of cultural policy alongside the typical political economic concerns in the urban politics and development lit- eratures. Our framework synthesizes several research streams that combine in global factors driving the articulation of culture into city politics. This frames our studies of the local processes through which this articulation occurs on the ground in Toronto.

articles.title: Romantic Partner Search: The Endogenous-Horizon Optimal Stopping Problem with Somatic Maintenance Trade-offs
articles.authors: Axel Schumacher
articles.abstract: Loneliness is a population-scale mortality risk, and its time-cost bites especially hard for adults who hope to build a family under a biological deadline. Individuals pursuing aggressive somatic optimization face a time-allocation trade-off that can be formalized through sequential search mathematics. Every hour spent on somatic maintenance, whether sleep protocols, training, nutrition, or longevity biohacking, extends the search horizon for a romantic partner and typically raises mate value. Every same hour is an hour not spent meeting people, so the rate at which new candidates arrive falls in direct proportion to the time spent in isolation. The body being optimized is useless to the project without someone to share it with. We formalize this trade-off by joining optimal stopping theory with life-history biology. Three results follow. First, the optimal allocation is bounded strictly below the full daily budget: even at arbitrarily high intervention efficacy (the "longevity escape velocity" limit), a rational searcher devotes no more than 50% of daily bandwidth to somatic maintenance in the symmetric-linear case, and less under diminishing biological returns. Beyond that line, isolation destroys more romantic value than infinite time can ever recover. Second, the asymmetric clock of female fecundity forces reservation thresholds to fall on a steep Riccati curve. Fertility preservation then becomes a high-stakes wager: it pays off when deployed early and with high clinic efficacy, and it backfires when deployed late or on low-efficacy interventions. Third, the model's abstract arrival rate λ maps onto an empirical, measurable construct, the Social Connectivity Value (SCV) framework (Schumacher, 2026), which specifies the weekly social floor that must remain protected and the corresponding cap on somatic investment. The prescription is precise: invest seriously in health, cap that investment at the (k−1)/(2k) ceiling derived below, and protect a weekly minimum of real-world social exposure as non-negotiable infrastructure. Longevity must serve the goal of flourishing, not become a sterile substitute for it.

articles.title: Estimation of exact months for the microdata and rolling quarter series from PNAD Contínua
articles.authors: Marcos Dantas Hecksher; Rogério J. Barbosa
articles.abstract: Brazil’s Continuous National Household Sample Survey (PNADC) publishes labor market indicators for rolling quarters, concealing the precise timing and magnitude of economic shocks. This article presents two complementary methods for recovering genuine monthly estimates. The microdata approach exploits the rotating panel design and respondent birthdates to deterministically identify the exact reference month of each interview, achieving a 97% determination rate across 28.4 million observations (2012–2025) with monthly samples 51% larger than those of the old Monthly Employment Survey (PME). The aggregate approach inverts the rolling-quarter averaging formula for over 80 official indicators published by the Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics (IBGE). Validation uses minimum wage transitions: the predictable one-month lag between habitual and effective income confirms identification accuracy across 16 adjustments. Applications show that COVID-19 peak unemployment was 2 percentage points higher than quarterly data indicate, that monthly poverty captures the precise Auxílio Emergencial effect, and that weekly data reveal differentiated carnival impacts across metropolitan regions. Both methods are implemented in an open-source R package.

articles.title: The Retreat from DEI: Language Change on Large U.S. Foundation Websites, 2019–2025
articles.authors: Chad M. Topaz; Benham Cobb; Frances Lu; Aniya Smith; Nathan Zekarias
articles.abstract: Following the murder of George Floyd in 2020, many U.S. organizations, including large private foundations, publicly foregrounded language related to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI). Beginning in 2023, a series of legal and political developments shifted the institutional environment: the Supreme Court’s ruling in Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President and Fellows of Harvard College (2023), state-level anti-DEI legislation, and President Trump’s Executive Order 14151 (2025). Using the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine, we constructed a longitudinal corpus of archived web pages from thirteen large U.S. private foundations with established DEI vocabulary and tracked the frequency of sixty-seven DEI-related terms from 2019 to 2025. Among nine foundations with paired 2023 and 2025 data, six reduced their DEI vocabulary intensity, and the decline spanned many DEI terms rather than concentrating in a few. The aggregate decline across the full sixty-seven-term index is marginal (Wilcoxon p = .071, one-sided). It is more pronounced when restricting the dictionary to the construct’s definitional core—the diversity-equity-inclusion triad and its morphological variants (p = .030). These patterns are suggestive of institutional isomorphism, with foundations appearing to converge on retreat from DEI language amid shared shifts in the political and legal environment, though our descriptive design cannot adjudicate among mimetic convergence, normative diffusion, and parallel independent responses to a shared political climate. The findings establish an empirical baseline for tracking changes in organizational communication about equity during a period of political pressure.

articles.title: Epistemic Infrastructure Under Attack: Conceptualizing the Capture and Destruction of Public Knowledge Institutions
articles.authors: Zarine Kharazian; Benjamin Mako Hill
articles.abstract:
From organized disinformation campaigns on social media to politically motivated
attempts to take over publishers and platforms, the last decade has seen a proliferation
of attacks on the epistemic infrastructure that people rely on to understand the world. We
argue that scholars, practitioners, and policymakers have struggled to respond effectively to
these threats in part because they lack systematic ways to understand the shifting nature of
what is under threat and how it is being attacked. Drawing on Elinor Ostrom’s classic work
on commons governance, we develop a conceptual framework that conceptualizes epistemic
infrastructure as a shared resource system whose three interdependent components—
knowledge, governance, and legitimacy—can each be attacked independently by motivated
actors through two distinct modes: capture and destruction. Applied to recent attacks on
Wikipedia, public broadcasters, and government data agencies, the framework reveals how
attacks frequently target governance structures and institutional legitimacy rather than the
knowledge outputs and information flows that existing approaches focus on, with implications
for how scholars and practitioners diagnose and respond to these threats.

articles.title: The patterns of academic mobility and research collaboration in Latin America & Caribbean and Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.authors: Huilin Ge; Dmitry Kochetkov
articles.abstract: This study provides a comparative analysis of the international mobility, collaboration networks, disciplinary structures, and authorship roles of researchers from Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) and Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) from 1990 to 2023. Our findings show that both regions maintain long-standing ties with Europe and North America, but also exhibit a significant rise in intra-regional collaboration, particularly in SSA, where regional partnerships now exceed those with Europe and Central Asia. Engagement with East Asia and the Pacific has expanded as well, especially in SSA. Disciplinary patterns reveal persistent differences between SSH and STEM fields, with SSH maintaining higher shares of solo authorship but gradually increasing collaboration. Finally, authorship analysis indicates that although first-author roles decline in international collaborations, last-author positions have grown, especially in SSA, suggesting a gradual strengthening of senior research leadership. Together, these findings highlight evolving forms of scientific integration and capacity building across two key Global South regions.

articles.title: Health is Timed: Temporal practices and the making of health and care in early parenthood
articles.authors: Christian Bröer; Zana Costa Cosac; Gerben Moerman; Gerlieke Veltkamp; Soely Mandrone
articles.abstract:
Health is timed. This article develops temporal practices as a concept for analysing how health and care are made in and through time in early parenthood. Drawing on longitudinal ethnographic research with 58 families in Amsterdam (278 interviews and 77 observations since 2016), we show how temporal practices shape variable health experiences like fatigue, worry, calm, recovery, and bonding in relation to care demands, bodily rhythms, and institutional schedules.
We identify four dynamically related temporal practices: projecting (organising the present through future-oriented health prevention and aspirations), carrying (bringing past health experiences into present care), ordering (planning, sequencing and synchronising days and weeks), and pacing (accelerating, slowing down, waiting and improvising when ordering fails). Rather than treating stress or relaxation as simple outcomes of time scarcity, we show how parents increasingly work on temporal order through routines, calendars, techniques of calming down, and timed exceptions, seeking to stabilise sleep, recovery and everyday care. These temporal patterns can support wellbeing, but they can also generate new burdens through gendered coordination labour when rhythms misalign and repairs are required.
We argue that temporal practices function as organising practices that connect and separate health practices across domains (sleep, feeding, paid work, and relationship repair). We conclude by proposing time-care as a practice-based intervention orientation: shifting from individual time management toward reshaping the temporal conditions of care (childcare, workplace rhythms, professional support) under which recovery and prevention become possible.

articles.title: Building a Shock–Crisis Index: Development and Initial Validation of a Diagnostic Tool
articles.authors: Mehmet Oktemgil; Murat Karacay
articles.abstract: Management research still lacks a disciplined way to distinguish a severe external interruption from a deeper condition of organisational deterioration. Existing literature on shocks, crises, disruption, and resilience captures parts of that problem, but it often blurs trigger, condition, response, and outcome. This paper develops and initially validates the Shock–Crisis Index (SCI), a theory-led diagnostic architecture designed to distinguish bounded post-shock disruption from a more persistent, stock-eroding organisational condition. The SCI is built around three proposed condition-forming dimensions—temporal persistence, stock erosion, and adaptive capacity—and is implemented as a formative composite rather than a reflective latent scale. The empirical setting is a panel of 3,104 listed U.S. firms observed around the COVID-19 disruption, used here as a common adverse setting for initial validation rather than as a clean causal identification environment. The evidence indicates that the SCI generates meaningful dispersion, exhibits limited indicator redundancy, remains broadly stable across alternative weighting specifications, and aligns with independent fragility and recovery markers, including Altman Z-prime, distress probability, recovery duration, and composite distress signals. These findings provide initial support for a condition-based diagnostic approach to the shock–crisis boundary, while also clarifying that the present study is stronger on classificatory coherence than on full causal adjudication. The paper contributes by reframing crisis as a post-disturbance organisational condition with distinct dynamics, by offering a formative measurement architecture for diagnosing that condition, and by providing a first empirical validation that can be tested and refined across other exogenous shock settings.

articles.title: Language Disparities in Moderation Workforce Allocation by Social Media Platforms
articles.authors: Manuel Tonneau; Diyi Liu; Ryan McGrady; Kevin Zheng; Ralph Schroeder; Ethan Zuckerman; Scott Hale
articles.abstract: Content moderation is one of the earliest and most consequential large-scale applications of artificial intelligence, shaping both online safety and the boundaries of permissible speech. In practice, moderation operates as a sociotechnical human–AI system, combining automated flagging with human review, as automated systems remain too error-prone to operate independently. Leveraging newly mandated transparency disclosures under the European Union's Digital Services Act (DSA), we conduct the first cross-platform audit of human content moderation workforce allocation across languages. Across six major platforms, we uncover substantial cross-lingual disparities in both language coverage and moderator staffing relative to the volume of user-generated content. While larger platforms such as YouTube and Meta employ moderators for many languages, millions of EU-based users on smaller platforms, including Twitter/X, post in languages without any dedicated human oversight. Among covered languages, staffing is often highly disproportionate to content volume, with English consistently prioritized over widely spoken Global Majority languages such as Spanish, Portuguese, and Arabic. Where enforcement data permit analysis, we further document large cross-lingual disparities in individual moderator workload, with per-moderator daily decision volumes differing by more than an order of magnitude across languages. Together, these findings raise fundamental fairness concerns for both users and moderators, implying both an unequal user protection from online harms across linguistic communities, and an uneven distribution of the cognitive and emotional burdens of moderation labor. They further demonstrate that meaningful accountability for AI-assisted content moderation requires transparency not only about automated systems and enforcement outcomes, but about how human moderation capacity is allocated, justified, and sustained across languages.

articles.title: Institutional Corruption and Green Crime in Rural Ghana: A Social and Ecological Disorganisation Analysis of Gold Mining Under the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) Extractivist Regime
articles.authors: Raymond Appiah; KuoRayMao
articles.abstract: This chapter examines the intersection of institutional corruption and environmental crime in rural Ghana, focusing on gold extraction. Using a social and ecological disorganisation framework, it argues that the erosion of governance structures, shaped by colonial legacies and enduring legal dualism, has enabled environmental and social harm. Weak regulatory enforcement, widespread corruption among statutory and customary authorities, and limited state capacity intensify social and ecological breakdown. Chinese companies, particularly those linked to the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), exploit these disordered contexts to carry out environmentally destructive practices with little accountability. The resulting degradation disproportionately affects rural populations as collusion between multinational actors and local elites further weakens resistance and undermine collective governance in rural Ghana. Structural constraints, including conflicting land rights, jurisdictional overlap, and fragmented enforcement, impede efforts to address rural green crime. The chapter concludes that without addressing the systemic roots of social disorganisation and corruption, green crime will persist, driven by global mineral demand, deepening the marginalisation of rural communities and accelerating ecological decline in rural Ghana.

articles.title: Reconciling Diverging Meanings of Just Transitions in a Fragmented World
articles.authors: Rudeena Jabar; Arohi Patil; Anjali Sharma; Juhi Chatterjee
articles.abstract: Just transition, an integral part of climate action, has become increasingly popular in both academic discussions and global climate negotiations. In 2022, the Just Transition Work Programme (JTWP) was established with the aim of discussing pathways to just transition in a climate-constrained world. However, the negotiations at JTWP have been slow due to fundamental disagreements in the interpretation of just transition between the Global North and Global South countries. The just transition (JT) scholarship has paid limited attention to explain the reasons for the starkly different understandings of JT across countries. In this paper, we first review academic literature on JT frameworks to understand whether and how they discuss the tensions that emerge when applying JT theories in practice. As we find limited academic literature on this topic, we develop a conceptual framework that focuses on highlighting and understanding the tensions that emerge in operationalizing academic JT frameworks in the real world. In particular, we focus on explaining tensions across different types of justice, geographical scales, and time. We then apply this framework to analyze JTWP negotiation proceedings. Our findings suggest that the existing justice frameworks do not consider the interactions between different types of justice, geographies, and time scales. This often leads them to ignore the tensions that could arise and the trade-offs that are involved in achieving a comprehensive vision of justice for all parts of the world at the same time. Through our framework, we are able to explore these tensions and explain the slow progress on JTWP negotiations. In the end, we provide examples to illustrate strategies that could be used to highlight and potentially resolve these tensions and, in turn, contribute to globally just low-carbon energy transitions.

articles.title: Job Quality, Work–Family Conflict, Anxiety, and Family Well-Being Among Young Workers in Singapore
articles.authors: Cameron Tan; Irene YH Ng; Xi Wen Chan; Gerard Chung
articles.abstract:
Objective: To examine associations between job quality, work–family conflict (WFC), generalised anxiety disorder (GAD), and family well-being (FWB) among young employees in Singapore.
Background: Work-related demands and resources may spill over across life domains, shaping individual strain and family functioning. Early adulthood involves overlapping career and family transitions that may heighten vulnerability to WFC. However, young workers remain underexamined, and much of the existing evidence derives from Western contexts. 
Methods: Data were drawn from Wave 3 of the In-Work Poverty study, comprising 677 full-time employees aged 21–35. Multidimensional job quality was measured using an adaptation of Eurofound’s Job Quality Index. Regression and path analyses examined associations between job quality, WFC, GAD, and FWB, adjusting for sociodemographic and occupational characteristics.
Results: Higher job quality was associated with lower levels of WFC and GAD, with progressively better outcomes observed from strained to highly resourced jobs. FWB was also higher in more resourced job categories, although associations were weaker and less consistent. WFC partially mediated the association between job quality and GAD, but not FWB. 
Conclusion: Multidimensional job quality was associated with mental health and family outcomes among young workers in a non-Western context. While WFC helps account for associations with GAD, the more complex pattern for FWB suggests that family outcomes may depend on additional relational and household processes beyond individual role strain.

articles.title: The Hidden Costs of Gatekeeping in Open Science: Quasi-Experimental Evidence from arXiv's Endorsement Policy Tightening
articles.authors: Wenchang Wang
articles.abstract: Open science platforms promise to democratize scholarly communication, yet many impose social network-dependent barriers to entry. We study the causal effect of arXiv's January 2026 endorsement policy tightening---which required new submitters to secure endorsement from established authors rather than relying on institutional affiliation alone---on new author entry rates. Constructing a panel of 143 subcategories observed weekly over 94 weeks from 3 million papers, we estimate Poisson quasi-maximum likelihood models with two-way fixed effects. The policy caused a 13--18% overall decline in new submitter rates, with catastrophic effects on disciplines with sparse co-authorship networks: economics (-49%) and quantitative biology (-49%) lost nearly half their new entrants, while well-connected fields like physics were unaffected. A formal interaction model confirms that pre-treatment co-authorship network density significantly buffers the policy shock (p = .005). Event study, staggered difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and a bioRxiv placebo test support causality. These findings reveal that access policies conditioning participation on pre-existing social networks systematically exclude researchers from peripheral communities, reinforcing the very inequalities open science purports to dismantle.

articles.title: Generation Matters: The Role of Migration and Generational Status for Distributive Justice Preferences
articles.authors: Margherita Cusmano
articles.abstract:
People judge existing economic inequality based on distributive justice principles such as merit,
equality, and need. The degree to which they support these principles—their distributive justice
preferences—and the ensuing judgment of inequality as just or unjust differ across social
groups. Scholars have identified social positions within the social structure, such as socio-economic status, as factors driving such differences. However, social structures are not sealed-off
systems. In a globalizing world, they are increasingly shaped by migration movements. So far,
relatively little attention has so far been paid to migration experiences as a factor potentially
underlying different perspectives on distributive justice. Integrating the insight from migration
scholarship that generation matters, this study explores migration background and migrants’
generational status as possible factors shaping distributive justice attitudes. Using an original
survey in Germany, this study tests whether migrants of different generations hold different
views than non-migrants. Almost no differences were found with regard to individuals who
moved to Germany as children or grew up in Germany with migrant parents. However, personal
experiences of migration in adolescence or adulthood strongly shape distributive justice preferences. Migrants who moved to Germany after or during adolescence tend to find inequality
more acceptable and support the equality and need principles less. The paper highlights migrants as an interesting population for empirical justice research. Moreover, it urges justice
scholars to differentiate between migrant generations.

articles.title: Do LLMs Read for Free? Auditing the Open Access Bias in AI-Mediated Science
articles.authors: Wenchang Wang
articles.abstract: How large language models affect various social activities has been widely discussed; however, how large language models themselves influence the mechanisms of scientific knowledge production remains unclear. To investigate this question, this paper designs a natural experiment based on a large-scale bibliometric dataset. Using a Difference-in-Differences model with Poisson Pseudo-Maximum Likelihood estimation and analyzing citation records from 64,841 articles across 120 communication journals, I find that the emergence of large language models amplifies the visibility gap between Open Access (OA) and non-OA articles. Specifically, OA articles experienced a significant post-ChatGPT citation acceleration of approximately 0.84% per month, accumulating to roughly 10% over 12 months. Importantly, this estimate represents a conservative lower bound, as gradual LLM adoption and journal review lags attenuate the measured treatment intensity. The findings suggest that while large language models promote knowledge dissemination, they also exacerbate inequality in academic knowledge production.

articles.title: Urban Science Beyond Samples: Up-to-Date Street Network Models and Indicators for Every Urban Area in the World
articles.authors: Geoff Boeing
articles.abstract: Urban planners need up-to-date, global, and consistent street network models and indicators to measure resilience and performance, model accessibility, and target local quality-of-life interventions. This article presents up-to-date street network models and indicators for every urban area in the world. It uses 2025 urban area boundaries from the Global Human Settlement Layer, allowing users to join these data to hundreds of other urban attributes. Its workflow ingests 180 million OpenStreetMap nodes and 360 million OpenStreetMap edges across 10,351 urban areas in 189 countries. The code, models, and indicators are publicly available for reuse. These resources unlock worldwide urban street network science beyond samples as well as local analyses in under-resourced regions where models and indicators are otherwise less-accessible.

articles.title: Evaluating Education for Biodiversity: A Scoping Review of Practices and Frameworks
articles.authors: Alix Cosquer; Olivier Gimenez; Valérie De-Saint-Vaulry; Charlotte Francesiaz
articles.abstract:
Education and public engagement are increasingly recognized as essential levers for biodiversity conservation. Over the past decade, biodiversity-related education and engagement initiatives have expanded considerably, yet their evaluation remains fragmented and methodologically uneven. This diversity complicates assessments of effectiveness, comparability, and long-term conservation relevance.
We conducted a scoping review of peer-reviewed empirical studies published between 2015 and 2025 to examine how evaluation practices are designed, implemented, and reported in biodiversity-related education and engagement initiatives. Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, 74 articles were selected from the Web of Science Core Collection and analyzed in terms of implementation contexts, target publics, educational practices, evaluation objectives, methodologies, temporalities, reported outcomes, limitations, and levers.
The review reveals a strong concentration of evaluations in formal education settings, primarily targeting children and young people. Objectives are largely structured around the knowledge–attitudes–behaviors (KAB) framework, with limited attention to competencies, empowerment, collective processes, or ecological outcomes. Most studies rely on short-term, self-reported quantitative measures, while longitudinal, qualitative, participatory, and process-oriented approaches remain underrepresented. Although experiential, place-based, creative, and participatory practices are identified as promising levers, their impacts are rarely assessed beyond immediate individual-level outcomes.
Overall, the findings expose a structural tension between the diversity of biodiversity education practices and the absence of shared, integrative evaluation frameworks. Advancing evaluation in this field requires systemic, reflexive, and multi-scalar approaches linking individual learning, collective action, institutional change, and conservation outcomes. Such a shift is essential to strengthen the contribution of education to conservation and to support more robust, cumulative, and policy-relevant evidence.

articles.title: Instruments and Artefacts: Mapping the Adoption of Large Language Models in Social Sciences
articles.authors: Bogdan Mamaev; Qizhou Wang; Matteo Vergani; Christopher Leckie
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly reshaping social science research. This study analyses a corpus of 12,223 papers (2019–2026) to identify methodological shifts and disciplinary divergences. Using a hybrid approach combining LLM-based quantitative surveys with BERTopic thematic analysis, we trace how these models are employed across social science fields. Our findings reveal exponential growth in LLM adoption, dominated by proprietary models - primarily OpenAI’s GPT family via commercial APIs. We establish a divide between tool-oriented disciplines, which utilise LLMs for data extraction and prediction, and reflective-critical disciplines, which interrogate models as cultural artifacts shaped by bias. We document a transition from simple text classification to advanced applications in simulation, text-based modelling, and synthetic data generation. However, these developments have outpaced rigorous evaluative frameworks, creating a "validation gap." Reliance on black-box LLMs poses challenges for reproducibility, transparency, and measurement stability. We conclude that for the social sciences to successfully integrate LLMs, the field must move beyond reliance on commercial providers and prioritise domain-specific standards for validation and reporting, and adopt more open alternatives.

articles.title: Friends on the Other Side: Receptiveness to Opposing Views Predicts Formation of Politically Heterogeneous Relationships
articles.authors: Brian Philip Reschke; Julia A. Minson; Hannah Riley Bowles; Mathijs de Vaan; Sameer Srivastava
articles.abstract: Growing evidence points to the corrosive influence of partisanship on society. We examine the relationship between “receptiveness to opposing views” (i.e., an individual difference in the willingness to engage with information from opposing perspectives) and the propensity to form positive and close collaborative relationships with ideologically opposed others. Across three studies varying in sampling and methodological approaches—a retrospective network study, a time-lagged field study, and an experiment—we find that individual and mutual receptiveness mitigate the negative influence of ideological opposition on relationship formation and willingness to collaborate. We find evidence that receptiveness is distinctively influential as compared to related personality characteristics (e.g., extraversion, self-monitoring, agreeableness, openness, intellectual humility). These findings contribute to our understanding of how individual differences shape social network formation and collaboration across ideological divides.

articles.title: Measuring Corruption from Text Data
articles.authors: Arieda Muço
articles.abstract: Using Brazilian municipal audit reports, I construct an automated corruption index that combines a dictionary of audit irregularities with principal component analysis. The index validates strongly against independent human coders, explaining 71–73 % of the variation in hand-coded corruption counts in samples where coders themselves exhibit high agreement, and the results are robust within these validation samples. The index behaves as theory predicts, correlating with municipal characteristics that prior research links to corruption. Supervised learning alternatives yield nearly identical municipal rankings (R2=0.98), confirming that the dictionary approach captures the same underlying construct. The method scales to the full audit corpus and offers advantages over both manual coding and Large Language Models (LLMs) in transparency, cost, and long-run replicability.

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence, Value-Based Care, and Episode-Based Payment Reform in the United States: A Policy Case for Accountable Adoption
articles.authors: Laurina Wiley
articles.abstract: Value-based care remains one of the clearest payment and delivery reforms available to the United States healthcare system. It pays for prevention, coordination, quality, and responsible use of resources rather than volume alone. Artificial intelligence can support that shift when organizations use it to identify actionable risk, make episodes of care easier to understand, improve navigation, reduce avoidable administrative work, and connect clinical decisions to measurable outcomes (Topol, 2019). This policy review supports broader use of value-based care, bundled payments, and episode-based analytics, while arguing that adoption needs careful governance. The main point is simple: artificial intelligence should not be treated as a stand-alone technology purchase. It should be used as infrastructure for accountable care. The paper links data infrastructure, risk prediction, bundled payment design, episode definitions, provider performance measurement, care coordination, economic evaluation, equity monitoring, and institutional accountability. Episode-based payment and bundled payment models are especially useful because they show the full course of care, encourage coordination across settings, and give artificial intelligence tools a practical target: forecasting cost, finding quality gaps, and prompting timely intervention.

articles.title: Polarized but still connected: the role of ethical anchoring in aligning multivocality and empathy in WhatsApp political discussions during wartime
articles.authors: Adi Gnapp; Yael Ben David; Paul Condon; Nava Levit-Binnun
articles.abstract: Political discussions increasingly take place on digital platforms, yet little is known about the conditions under which constructive dialogue can take place and sustain over time. While prior research has examined the properties of constructive online political discussions, few studies explore how this type of dialogue evolves in large and politically diversed online groups, particularly during moments of acute polarization. This study identifies the conditions under which constructive political talk was enabled and sustained in a WhatsApp group of about 600 Israeli dharma and mindfulness practitioners during the first two months of the Gaza war following the October 7 attacks. A qualitative discourse analysis of 27 threads (885 messages; 73,347 words) traced how participants navigated moral disagreement in real time. Early exchanges were marked by accusation, but empathetic and reflective styles gradually emerged. Based on these findings, we propose a three-layer process model in which an ontological foundation valuing diversity legitimizes the cultivation of ethical anchoring practices, which are operationalized through invocations of compassion and right speech. Findings illuminate mechanisms in which digital communities establish and sustain constructive dialogue in polarized times.

articles.title: A Pós-Graduação na UFABC: duas décadas de interdisciplinaridade, excelência e inclusão
articles.authors: Charles Morphy D. Santos; João Paulo Gois
articles.abstract: Este artigo analisa a trajetória de quase duas décadas da pós-graduação da Universidade Federal do ABC (UFABC), destacando sua evolução desde a criação, em 2006. Estruturada sobre os pilares da interdisciplinaridade, da excelência e da inclusão, a instituição expandiu-se de cinco programas de pós-graduação (PPGs) stricto sensu iniciais em 2007 para 29 PPGs e 11 cursos lato sensu em 2026.  Entre os diferenciais estratégicos da pós-graduação da UFABC estão o Doutorado Acadêmico Industrial (DAI) e o Mestrado Acadêmico para Inovação (MAI), que integram a pesquisa acadêmica às demandas do setor produtivo e do ecossistema de inovação. Além disso, o forte processo de internacionalização da pesquisa da UFABC foi impulsionado pelo programa CAPES-PrInt e deve continuar a ser fomentado com a aprovação da rede coordenada pela instituição no programa CAPES Global.Edu.  A maturidade acadêmica da pós-graduação da UFABC é evidenciada pelo seu desempenho nas duas últimas avaliações quadrienais da CAPES (2021-2022 e 2025). Particularmente na avaliação mais recente, 40% dos PPGs subiram e nenhum teve diminuição de nota. O artigo também enfatiza o compromisso social da universidade por meio da institucionalização de políticas de ações afirmativas e de permanência estudantil, garantindo a inclusão de grupos sub-representados na pesquisa de alto nível.

articles.title: Gen-Entertainment: Towards a Framework for Trans-Platform Discovery, Hybrid Audience–Creator Roles, and Socially Embedded Participation in Digital Entertainment
articles.authors: Olli Kallioinen
articles.abstract:
Digital entertainment behavior is increasingly trans-platform, role-fluid, and socially embedded — yet no existing framework treats these tendencies as one connected formation. This paper proposes Gen-Entertainment (Gen-E) as a provisional conceptual framework for a cross-category behavioral formation organized through three linked dimensions: trans-platform discovery, hybrid audience–creator roles, and socially embedded participation across streaming, social, and gaming environments. Drawing on structured literature synthesis, it argues that entertainment discovery increasingly begins outside the environment of eventual consumption, that a growing share of users inhabit a hybrid zone between stable audiencehood and legible creatorhood, and that entertainment increasingly functions as infrastructure for social coordination and belonging. 

Critically, the synthesis suggests that within the Gen-E formation, behavioral type is a more analytically coherent predictor of entertainment practice than age cohort: a high-engagement older user may cluster behaviorally with younger peers more than with their own generational cohort, a pattern consistent with substantial evidence from adjacent domains showing that behavioral variables outperform age in explaining digital media behavior. Gen-E is not proposed as a validated segment or confirmed typology, but as a theoretically grounded construct for a behavioral object that participatory-culture theory, platform-logics scholarship, media-repertoire research, and generational frameworks currently illuminate only in parts.

articles.title: HCBfT: A High-Cognitive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Turkish
articles.authors: EMRE KIZILKAYA; Kerem Rızvanoğlu
articles.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) approach human-level performance on traditional English-centric benchmarks, evaluating their capabilities in morphologically rich and resource-poor languages has become a critical challenge. Existing Turkish benchmarks frequently rely on direct translations of English datasets or basic arithmetic tasks stripped of semantic context, failing to capture true linguistic and cultural comprehension. We introduce the High-Cognitive Benchmark for Turkish (HCBfT) and its inaugural version, TR200. This private dataset comprises 200 multiple-choice adversarial questions. To construct it, we combined original items with questions distilled from an initial pool of more than 300,000 standardized test questions. Eliminating redundant and low-difficulty items provided an agile, cost-efficient and highly discriminative evaluation framework. HCBfT-TR200 evaluates advanced native-level comprehension, complex verbal logic, non-standard dialects, cultural alignment and multidisciplinary domain knowledge specific to Turkey. We keep the dataset strictly private to ensure contamination-free evaluation. The original questions resist simple information retrieval. We filtered all candidate items through a diverse panel of frontier LLMs to identify the most challenging subsets (where more than 75% of models failed) and augmented them with human-authored adversarial traps. We evaluated 20 mid-sized and frontier models (10 API, 10 local). A general educated adult human baseline, established through a controlled administration to 24 native Turkish-speaking undergraduates under an anchored incomplete block design, yielded 37.9% accuracy. Results demonstrate that HCBfT-TR200 presents a significant challenge to LLMs. The commercial leader, Gemini 3 Flash, achieved 68.0% accuracy. Mistral Large 3, the open-weight model by Paris-based Mistral AI, ranked second at 52.5% while costing $0.07 per correct answer. The top local open-weight model, Gemma 4 31B IT, achieved 42.0%. Ministral 3 14B Reasoning led in hardware efficiency with a ratio of 6.7 correct answers per gigabyte (CPG). By providing a standardized metric for open-weight and API models, HCBfT serves as a reliable guide for the secure and efficient deployment of culturally aligned, locally hosted AI systems in the Turkish public and private sectors. Future iterations of the benchmark will broaden this framework beyond multiple-choice evaluation to rigorously assess open-ended generation, instruction following and culturally context-aware persona adoption.

articles.title: Young carers and educational attainment in late adolescence: a nationally representative analysis of post-16 education in England
articles.authors: Alejandra Letelier; Anne McMunn; Andy McGowan; Krista Cartlidge; Rebecca E Lacey
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Young carers, defined as individuals <18 years providing unpaid care for someone with disabilities, health conditions, or addiction, are a frequently overlooked group. In the UK, around 12% of young people report caring responsibilities, yet no national studies have examined their post-16 educational attainment outcomes. This study assessed associations between young caring and attainment at Key Stage 5 (age 16-18 years) in England and whether these varied by sociodemographic factors and care characteristics.

Methods: Data were drawn from Understanding Society linked to the National Pupil Database in England (2009–2018). The analytic sample included 3,306 adolescents aged 17–18 years. Caring status was self-reported; attainment outcomes were obtained from administrative records. Regression models adjusted for demographic and socioeconomic factors. Additional analyses examined care recipient and care intensity plus modification by gender, ethnicity and socioeconomic position.

Results: Young carers had lower educational attainment than their peers. Compared with non-carers, they obtained fewer qualifications (A-Level and equivalent passes, IRR = 0.88, 95% CI 0.79–0.98), were less likely to achieve two or more A-Levels at grades A*–E (OR = 0.67, 95% CI 0.48–0.93) and scored lower on academic and A-Level point totals. In addition, they were also more likely to follow a vocational pathway (OR = 1.40, 95% CI 1.03–1.92). Attainment gaps were larger among boys and higher care intensity (≥10 hours/week).

Conclusions: Young carers face persistent educational attainment inequalities at the end of compulsory education, particularly those providing intensive care and boys, highlighting the need for early identification and targeted support.

articles.title: Cross-Disciplinary Lessons for Improving Informed Consent Procedures in Research
articles.authors: Derek Robey; Hannah Baron; Sebastian Karcher; Dessislava Kirilova; Justine Davis; John Baumann; Rebecca Campbell; Scott Desposato; Roma Dhamanaskar; James DuBois
articles.abstract: Informed consent shouldn’t be reduced to a checkbox. Treating consent as an ongoing, participant‑centered process of engagement that is clearer, tailored to research context, and transparent about data, can strengthen ethical research and trust across both biomedical and social science disciplines, as they learn from each other.

articles.title: Quantifying the reproductive labor of pregnancy and pregnancy loss in the demographic study  of population health
articles.authors: Payal Hathi
articles.abstract: Pregnancy loss remains a neglected topic within demography, overlooking the significant costs of women’s reproductive labor globally. Using data from Demographic and Health Survey reproductive calendars across 65 countries, this analysis moves beyond live birth to describe the prevalence of pregnancy loss that women across countries experience. The analysis first quantifies the substantial number of person-months that women spend being pregnant in both pregnancies that end in live birth and those that do not. It then calculates a measure of maternal cumulative prevalence of pregnancy loss, demonstrating that women’s experience of pregnancy loss is common worldwide. Next, following the methodology of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR), the analysis calculates a parallel measure called the Total Pregnancy Rate (TPR) to show the average number of pregnancies a woman would experience if she survived through the end of the reproductive years and experienced at each age the period age- specific pregnancy rates. The TPR ranges from 4% to 115% larger than the TFR across countries, with an average of 16% larger. Given that pregnancy loss is known to be undermeasured in many places, these are likely lower bound magnitudes. Finally, cross-country variation in pregnancy loss shows no discernable relationship with potential determinants of pregnancy loss such as abortion incidence rates, per capita health expenditure, rates of anemia prevalence, or TFR, demonstrating the limits of existing fertility frameworks. Counting and considering women’s reproductive labor in the form of pregnancy, whether it ends in a live birth or not, is crucial to understanding women’s and population health, and for gaining a more complete picture of the underlying costs of demographic measures of fertility.

articles.title: The Limits of BPE Tokenization in Polish: Segmentation-Flexional Forms, Grammatical Anchoring, and First-Person Stability in Inflectional Language Models
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article analyzes BPE tokenization in Polish as a test case for a broader problem: the limits of statistical segmentation in an inflectional language. The starting point is the question of whether, and to what extent, frequency-based tokenization preserves units that are relevant for the linguistic description of Polish: orthographic form, the segmentation-phonemic level, syllable structure, logotomes, prefixes, derivational bases, inflectional endings, grammatical form, and the position of the speaking subject.
The empirical material includes the tokenization of diagnostic words, a simple children’s text, selected forms from the Preamble to the Constitution of the Republic of Poland, word-family tests, and examples containing Polish diacritics and nasal vowels. The analysis shows that BPE tokenizers may locally produce segments that coincide with syllabic, logotomic, or morphologically interpretable divisions, but they remain fundamentally dependent on the frequency of written forms. They do not systematically move from orthographic representation to the segmentation-phonemic level or to context-dependent phonetic realization.
The results also indicate that BPE stabilizes not grammatical categories themselves, but frequent surface fragments of their exponents. Segments such as -y, -ich, -ni, or -ć may appear as stable tokens or parts of tokens, but this does not amount to preserving the full grammatical form. A form such as ustanawiamy is not merely a sequence ending in -y; it is a verbal form anchored in conjugation, person, number, tense, mood, and aspect.
The article develops the concept of grammatical form anchoring, arguing that in Polish a form may not only encode syntactic relations, but also establish the position of the speaking subject. In forms such as poszłam, zrobiłam, or byłam, the grammatical “I” need not be expressed by a separate pronoun, because it is anchored in inflection. In interaction with AI, this reveals an additional problem: a language model does not possess its own stable grammatical “I”, but reconstructs it contextually, often mirroring the user’s forms or shifting grammatical gender.
I propose treating Rocławski’s segmentation-flexional forms not as a description of how BPE operates, but as a diagnostic framework for assessing the quality of tokenization boundaries. The findings lead to the research hypothesis that more stable modeling of Polish may require several layers: sublexical stabilization inspired by Rocławski’s system, anchoring grammatical form in the inflectional system, representing sentence patterns and verbal valency structure, and maintaining the position of the grammatical “I” in dialogue. From this perspective, the problem of Polish AI is not reducible to reducing the number of tokens. Rather, it concerns maintaining relations between orthographic representation, word form, grammatical function, sentence position, and the position of the speaking subject.

articles.title: Which Businesses Answer Surveys? Evidence from Dutch Administrative Data
articles.authors: Jack Fitzgerald
articles.abstract: I leverage a unique administrative register covering the universe of establishments in the Netherlands to examine how characteristics differ between establishments that do and do not respond to business surveys. Only 19% of Dutch establishments responded to regional business surveys in 2022. Responsive establishments employ fewer people, and exhibit higher parttime employment rates, than unresponsive establishments. Sectoral response rates vary by up to 32 percentage points. Enterprises registered to residential addresses comprise over 70% of establishments, yet exhibit response rates 18 percentage points lower than an average office. However, controlling for contact probability reveals that the majority of sectoral and occupational variation in response rates can be traced back to differences in contact probability rather than responsiveness. These findings highlight challenges in the representativeness and generalizability of business survey data, as well as opportunities to improve the design of business surveys.

articles.title: The Effect of a Woman-Friendly Occupation on Employment: U.S. Postmasters Before World War II
articles.authors: Sophie Li
articles.abstract: This paper studies U.S. postmasters in the early twentieth century, a rare woman-friendly occupation at a time when women’s employment opportunities were highly constrained. Using a novel dataset linking postmaster appointments to census records, I show that these well-paid, flexible positions attracted educated and qualified White women into the labor force. However, exploiting exogenous variation in reappointment probabilities through an RD design, I find that many women exited the labor force once their appointments ended. Few transitioned into other skilled occupations, accounting for only one-third of the 24 pp. employment loss. The results are consistent with a reservation-wage mechanism: women who secured alternative full-time skilled jobs with wages comparable to postmasters remained employed, while others stopped working.  The scarcity of jobs similar to postmasters likely limited the long-term gains of this otherwise advantageous occupation.

articles.title: Autism and Puberty: A Mixed Methods Systematic Review
articles.authors: Harriet Axbey; Rebecca Ellis; Madeleine Sinfield; Ellen Firth; Sebastian Charles Keith Shaw; Aaliyah Peeroo; Ben Carter; Aimee Grant
articles.abstract:
Background
Puberty is a time of profound change for all. Autistic children may find pubertal changes harder to navigate, due to sensitives surrounding sensory experiences, and difficulty with change. Our prospectively registered mixed methods systematic review (Registration: PROSPERO2023 CRD42023446750) aimed to synthesise research on Autism and puberty.
Methods
Included studies focused on puberty in Autistic people (including self-identifying) and those supporting them. Searches were limited to English language sources from 2013 - July 2023. We searched: Medline, APA PsycINFO, and CINAHL (all via EBSCOhost), and Web of Science (via Clarivate). Studies were subjected to Convergent Integrated synthesis (Joanna Briggs Institute). Quality assessments were undertaken using Joanna Briggs Institute tools.
Results
3,546 unique records were identified, and 61 items were included in the review. Most papers were high quality. We identified five themes. First, many studies compared Autistic and non-Autistic people, but few differences were identified, with puberty characterised as a universal experience. Second, parents felt unprepared for their child’s puberty, and uncertain of their future. Third, additional education in areas of puberty and sex were identified as needed for Autistic people, and those caring for them. Fourth, breaking norms was explored at length; with many parents worrying their child was not normal in behaviour and development. Finally described were physical changes of puberty and challenges these bring, specifically difficulties around menarche and menstruation. Limitations of studies included a lack of diversity (ethnicity; few fathers) and restricted consideration of confounding factors and author impact on research. 
Discussion 
Co-developed interventions are required, to facilitate understanding consent, navigating pubertal changes, and learning skills. The synthesised research did not focus on comfort and wellbeing, but more so on normality, or perceived lack of, of Autistic young people. Future research should prioritise topics of importance to Autistic people, and should seek consent from Autistic participants.

articles.title: Informational Power Through Pivotality: When a Hegemon May Choose Consensus
articles.authors: Manoel Galdino
articles.abstract: When does a powerful state prefer consensus to majority rule in an international organization? I develop a formal model of institutional design in which a hegemon has private information about the value of multilateral cooperation. Under majority rule, weaker states can form winning coalitions without the hegemon, so its private information has limited strategic value. Under unanimity, every agreement requires the hegemon's approval, forcing weaker states to decide how much to offer without knowing how valuable cooperation really is. This uncertainty works in the hegemon's favor: weaker states pay more than necessary to secure agreement, and majority rule eliminates this advantage entirely. In a calibration to OPEC circa 1986, the hegemon captures 28\% of the cooperation surplus under unanimity (9 percentage points above its outside option) compared to 23\% under majority (4 points above), a screening premium of 5 percentage points that majority rule eliminates entirely. This screening advantage means that, once cooperation is promising enough for weaker states to participate under unanimity, the hegemon strictly prefers it. Consensus, in this account, is not a concession to weaker states but an institutional arrangement that makes informational advantage politically productive---offering a distributive explanation for consensus rules in organizations such as OPEC.

articles.title: Governing online hate: The construction of “hateful ideology” in TikTok’s platform governance from 2018 to 2025
articles.authors: Yang Xu
articles.abstract: Social media platforms’ understanding of online hate is itself a governance choice with normative and political implications. This study examines the evolution of TikTok’s hate speech governance from 2018 to 2025, focusing on how the platform has conceptualized and deployed the governance concept of “hateful ideology” across its community guidelines, news releases, and transparency reports. Applying longitudinal document analysis (N=132), this study identified two major shifts in the platform’s hate speech policy: a 2020 conceptual shift that restructured hate speech around a four-category framework and a 2023 structural shift that reconfigured policy enforcement around a tiered visibility framework, with “hateful ideology” becoming a central organizing concept. TikTok’s present conception of hateful ideology combines a cognitivist framing of ideology (as belief systems) and a practice-based enforcement logic—a conceptual layering that produces interpretive discretion. The findings suggest that TikTok has primarily configured its concept of “hateful ideology” as a legitimation device calibrated to regulatory contexts, rather than a stable governance principle.

articles.title: Reconfiguring Teacher Agency in the Age of Generative AI: A Quasi-Experimental Study of the Relational Turn in Instructional Ecologies
articles.authors: XI ZHAN; Yuxiang Shi; Li Ma
articles.abstract: The rapid integration of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) into lesson design and instruction raises questions about how teachers’ instructional agency is reconfigured. This STEAM class-based quasi-experimental study involved 36 teachers and 462 students across three conditions: traditional teacher community, GAI-mediated group, and relational group. Grounded in Ihde’s (1990) human–technology relations theory, we combined quantitative analyses of student test performance and water-purification outcomes with video-based Lag Sequential Analysis of instructional behaviors. While student assessments showed no significant between-group differences, treating GAI as a partner rather than a tool reshaped teacher collaboration and classroom dynamics. Our findings suggest that human-GAI co-agency facilitates teaching and learning, and the impact of GAI is deeply embedded in the instructional ecology. A relational–ecological model of human–AI instructional agency and practical implications are discussed.

articles.title: Housing Prices and English Period Fertility
articles.authors: John Ermisch
articles.abstract: The aim of the study is to produce an estimate of the impact of house prices on English and Welsh period fertility during 2010-23, a period in which there were large declines in period fertility, large changes in house prices and considerable heterogeneity in regional variation of house prices.  It uses individual longitudinal data from Understanding Society (the first 14 waves), and it identifies impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities from variation in changes over time in prices within each region of residence. The estimates imply relatively large negative impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities. They can be interpreted as causal effects if two conditions hold: (1) there are no omitted time-varying regional variables correlated with house prices and fertility; and (2) women do not move to regions of lower prices in anticipation of future pregnancy.  It contributes to the literature by using data on individual women rather than area aggregates.  It also employs birth registration data to cross-validate the predictions of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR) and mean age at childbirth from the estimated model.

articles.title: From Bread to Bullets: Sectoral Differences in how Aid Changes the Geography of Conflict
articles.authors: Melanie Sauter
articles.abstract: How does humanitarian aid influence armed conflict dynamics, and how do its effects vary across sectors? This paper analyzes geocoded humanitarian projects and conflict events across Africa (2000–2024) to examine how sector-specific aid shapes territorial control and armed clashes. I argue that aid alters the strategic value of locations through two mechanisms: lootability (material extractability) and visibility (governance signaling). Using matched panel models and spatial grid-cell data, I compare conflict trends across areas with varying levels of aid exposure. Results show that aid increases territorial contestation and clashes. Food and water drive regional violence linked to access competition. Lootable aid produces universal competition, while governance-intensive aid destabilizes rebel-held zones. Violence against aid workers remains limited and largely opportunistic.

articles.title: The Cooperative Scope of Local Gods: Punishment, Omniscience, and Distant Cooperation among the Sihanaka of Madagascar
articles.authors: Anders Norge; Daniel Major-Smith; Benjamin Grant Purzycki
articles.abstract: Do supernatural beliefs in punishment and omniscience promote cooperation among distant unrelated individuals? Previous cross-cultural research – predominantly among “moralistic” religious traditions – suggests it might, although numerous open questions remain. Foremost among these are: “Do these results replicate among local religious traditions?” and “Is the relationship causal?” Here, we take a causally-informed approach to explore these questions among the Sihanaka from Madagascar. Our research question is: Do punishment and omniscience beliefs about a local god - zazavavindrano, an aquatic deity - promote cooperation towards co-religionists of this tradition? We assessed cooperation using Random Allocation and Dictator Games. Bayesian regression models were used, adjusting for plausible confounding factors, with g-computation methods to estimate our counterfactual causal effects of interest (i.e., the joint effect of zazavavindrano punishment and omniscience beliefs on cooperation with distant co-religionists). Across all games, conditions, and confounding assumptions, we found little evidence for a relationship between these zazavavindrano beliefs and cooperation. While zazavavindrano beliefs may not promote cooperation with unknown distant co-religionists, whether these beliefs facilitate cooperation in other domains – such as ecological management of wetlands, which is of greater concern to zazavavindrano than moral behaviour towards strangers – is an interesting open question for future research.

articles.title: An Astonishing Turn in the Attribution Debate on the Law of Comparative Advantage
articles.authors: Jorge Morales Meoqui
articles.abstract: The law of comparative advantage cannot be attributed to David Ricardo, James Mill, Robert Torrens or John Stuart Mill. Their writings, much like those of Adam Smith, strictly adhere to the classical rule for specialization, which asserts that one should acquire commodities abroad whenever offered cheaper than it would cost to produce them locally. They all viewed the cheaper price of foreign commodities as the logical starting point and condicio sine qua non of most exchanges between countries. Furthermore, the popular narrative about Ricardo’s alleged formulation of an alternative rule for specialization – the law or principle of comparative advantage – results from a misinterpretation of the well-known numerical example presented in Chapter 7 of his Principles of Political Economy and Taxation. John Stuart Mill is largely responsible for this misinterpretation; he obviously deserves no recognition or praise for that.

articles.title: Libraries as a ‘Third Place’ for Remote Workers: A Survey of Australian Workplace Preferences
articles.authors: Simon Wakeling; Philip Hider; Xi Wen Chan; Matthew J. Xerri
articles.abstract: As remote and hybrid work become more common, many workers are seeking ‘third places’ -alternative environments outside the home and office - to work and connect. This study explores the role of public libraries as alternative workplaces for remote workers in Australia. Drawing on survey data from 225 remote workers recruited via Prolific, we examine who uses libraries for remote work, how often, and why. Weekly, around eight per cent of respondents use libraries for at least half a day, a rate similar to that for coworking spaces, while a further one in three would consider doing so. Libraries are valued for quietness, affordability, internet access, and a sense of community, but limited privacy and restricted hours remain barriers. The findings highlight the library’s potential to meet workforce needs and offer practical implications for library managers seeking to design spaces and policies that better support remote workers and their wellbeing.

articles.title: Mary Pearcey's Final Message in the Spanish-Language Press, 1890–1891: From Madrid to Manila
articles.authors: Tina Jacobs; Alicia V. Donaldson
articles.abstract:
This research note presents the first systematic documentation of Spanish-language press coverage of the Mary Pearcey execution case across at least thirteen distinct newspapers spanning Madrid, Barcelona, and Manila, published between 23 December 1890 and 12 February 1891. Located primarily in the Hemeroteca Digital of the Biblioteca Nacional de España, these sources reveal that the Pearcey execution story was transmitted from London to Spain within hours via the London–Bilbao submarine telegraph cable, distributed by the Agencia Fabra (Spanish correspondent of the French Havas news agency), and published simultaneously on 23 December 1890 — the very day of execution — across at least nine Madrid daily newspapers spanning the full political spectrum: El Demócrata, El Diario Español, La Época, El Estandarte, La Iberia, La Opinión, El Siglo Futuro, El Siglo, and La Unión Católica. Two additional Madrid newspapers — El País and La República — published the dispatch the following day. La Vanguardia of Barcelona carried coverage on 28 December 1890. Most remarkably, the dispatch reached the Spanish colonial press, appearing in El Comercio (Manila) on 26 January 1891 and El Resumen (Manila) on 12 February 1891 — demonstrating that a Victorian British murder case traveled from London across three continents to the Philippines in approximately five weeks. All newspapers report Pearcey's final message: «No te he hecho traición» ("I have not betrayed you").
